parts.content:
Using an appropriate diagram, explain the potential impact of a 20% tariff on Indian-made solar panels on the equilibrium price and quantity in the EU market.

![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-55c37e40-4327-4a24-956f-e73146576730-1771840759346/5054235d-1539-4751-8c5d-518d35036c2d.jpeg)
parts.markscheme:
- The imposition of the tariff raises the price of imported solar panels in the EU, shifting the world price line upwards from $P_w$ to $P_w+t$. 
- As the price increases, domestic EU demand falls from $Q_4$ to $Q_3$, as some consumers find solar panels too expensive.
- Simultaneously, the higher price makes domestic production in the EU more viable, increasing quantity supplied by EU firms from $Q_1$ to $Q_2$.
- The total volume of imports falls from $(Q_4 - Q_1)$ to $(Q_3 - Q_2)$.
- The government earns tariff revenue (the rectangle area). Deadweight welfare loss occurs (the two shaded triangles) due to consumption distortion and production inefficiency.

<marks>2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks>4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content:
Using a market structure diagram, explain how government subsidies (PLI) to Indian solar cell exporters could potentially affect the Indian trade market.

![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-55c37e40-4327-4a24-956f-e73146576730-1771840759346/6844440a-7be0-47be-a2bf-53413fb3db01.jpeg)
parts.markscheme:
- Government subsidies lower the costs for Indian manufacturers, effectively shifting the domestic supply curve rightward (from $S_{d1}$ to $S_{d2}$).
- Producers receive a higher effective price ($P_w + s$), which encourages them to expand production from $Q_2$ to $Q_4$.
- At this higher price, domestic consumption in India may decrease from $Q_1$ to $Q_3$ (if the subsidy is focused purely on exports/production received price).
- Consequently, the surplus for export increases significantly from the original $(Q_2 - Q_1)$ to the new $(Q_4 - Q_3)$, allowing India to capture more global market share.

<marks>2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks>4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content:
Using a comparative advantage diagram, explain why India and Germany would benefit from free trade in green energy products.

![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-55c37e40-4327-4a24-956f-e73146576730-1771840759346/1ef2e78e-d2c2-49ee-a2c3-fe0fcaa38952.jpeg)
parts.markscheme:
- Germany has a comparative advantage in Precision Machinery due to advanced technological capital, while India has a comparative advantage in Solar Cells due to lower labor costs and scale of assembly.
- In the diagram, Germany's PPC has a lower opportunity cost for Machinery, and India's PPC has a lower opportunity cost for Solar Cells.
- By specializing in the product where they have the lowest opportunity cost and trading, both nations can consume at a point outside their individual PPCs, increasing global efficiency and total output of green technology.

<marks>2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks>4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content:
Using a circular flow of income diagram, explain how a narrowing of India’s current account deficit might affect its national income and economic growth.

![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-55c37e40-4327-4a24-956f-e73146576730-1771840759346/4106aa45-3306-4960-ad11-19ee70048bd4.jpeg)
parts.markscheme:
- The current account balance reflects the difference between exports (injections) and imports (leakages).
- A narrowing deficit means that either exports have increased or imports have decreased (or both), leading to a reduction in the net leakage from the circular flow of income.
- As leakages decrease relative to injections, the total volume of money circulating between households and firms increases through the multiplier effect.
- This leads to an increase in national income ($Y$) and stimulates economic growth.

<marks>2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks>4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using information from the text/data and your knowledge of economics, evaluate the effects of India–EU trade tensions and environmental tariffs on global economic interdependence.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

### **Definition**
- **Environmental Tariffs (CBAM)**: Taxes imposed on imported goods based on their carbon content to prevent carbon leakage and protect domestic firms following strict environmental rules.
- **Economic Interdependence**: The mutual reliance between countries for goods, services, technology, and capital.

### **Impact on Bilateral Interdependence**
- **Reduced Trade and Investment**: Table 1 shows trade fell from **€80B in 2019 to €72B in 2020** (a 10% drop), and investment fell sharply by **40%**. While there was some recovery by 2022 (€78B), the growth remains below the pre-tension potential.
- **Fragmentation of Supply Chains**: The 20% tariff on solar components forces EU firms to source from Vietnam/Thailand (trade diversion). This weakens the direct link between India and the EU but creates new dependencies elsewhere.

### **Technological Interdependence**
- **Vertical Linkages**: Despite trade barriers, India remains dependent on **German machinery** for production. The text states decoupling would take **over 15 years**.
- **Resource Dependency**: The EU relies on external processing of minerals and assembly labor. These deep structural links make total "de-risking" or decoupling very costly for both regions.

### **Role of Global Governance**
- **WTO Authority**: The tensions over India's PLI subsidies highlight the need for updated WTO rules. New rules requiring proof that subsidies aren't trade-distorting could strengthen rules-based interdependence and reduce unilateral protectionism (like CBAM).
- **Conflict of Interest**: There is a tension between **Free Trade (efficiency)** and **Environmental Protection (externalities)**. CBAM aims to fix a market failure (carbon footprint) but acts as a trade barrier.

### **Evaluation**
- **Short-term Disruption**: Tariffs and subsidies cause welfare loss and higher costs for green energy transition in the EU, potentially slowing global climate goals.
- **Long-term Resilience**: If trade barriers encourage India to develop its own precision machinery or the EU to become more competitive, it might lead to a more diversified and resilient (though perhaps less efficient) global system.
- **Trade Diversion**: The shift of production to Southeast Asia shows that global interdependence is difficult to eliminate; it merely reshapes into different geographic patterns.

### **Conclusion**
Trade tensions and environmental tariffs between India and the EU are causing a partial decoupling in bilateral trade. However, the structural dependence on specialized technology and the global nature of climate change mitigation ensure that economic interdependence remains high. The future of this relationship depends on whether the WTO can create a framework that balances fair competition with the urgent need for a global green transition.

questions.specification:
CASE STUDY: Economic Developments and Challenges in Azerbaijan

1. Azerbaijan, situated at the crossroads of Eastern Europe and Western Asia, has experienced significant economic reforms over the past decade. Historically reliant on hydrocarbons-mainly oil and natural gas—for export revenues, the country has sought to diversify its production base through agriculture, tourism, and technology sectors. In 2019, oil and gas accounted for about 85% of Azerbaijan's total exports, providing substantial government revenue but leaving the country vulnerable to global commodity price fluctuations.

2. In recent years, policymakers have introduced multiple initiatives to modernize infrastructure and reduce dependence on hydrocarbons. Between 2019 and 2022, the government invested over US$3 billion in roads, railways, and energy transmission lines, aiming to expand the country's capacity to produce and transport goods. According to the Statistical Committee. Azerbaijan's real GDP grew by 2.2% in 2020 and rebounded to 4.6% in 2022, partly due to a recovery in global oil prices. However, structural unemployment persists, especially in rural areas, where older agricultural practices lag behind modern production techniques.

3. Inflation, driven by rising global commodity costs, reached 8.4% in 2022, up from 2.6% in 2019. The Central Bank of Azerbaijan responded with conservative monetary policies that have stabilized the exchange rate of the Azerbaijani manat. Meanwhile, the government introduced tariffs on certain imported agricultural goods in 2021, aiming to protect local farmers from competition and stimulate domestic production. Critics argue that these tariffs lead to higher prices for consumers, while supporters believe they create incentives for farmers to modernize and invest in capital-intensive farming techniques.

4. Foreign direct investment (FDI) centered on the energy sector remains strong, although officials are eager to attract in manufacturing and services. Improvements in transport connectivity-facilitated by ongoing infrastructure projects-have encouraged discussions about further liberalizing trade regulations to boost exports of textiles, food products, and tech services. Yet logistical barriers at border checkpoints persist, contributing to delays and raising costs for exporters looking to access regional markets.

5. Socially, Azerbaijan has implemented targeted measures to address income inequality, including subsidies for utilities and food staples. Observers note that the impact of these subsidies can be uneven; while they help low-income households cope with rising prices, they can also create fiscal pressure if oil revenues decline. The government has recently introduced pilot programs that tie subsidies to specific income thresholds, with the objective of reducing misuse of public funds.

6. Despite ambitious diversification plans, the oil sector continues to dominate. Economic analysts warn that reliance on hydrocarbons could impede sustainable growth, especially if global oil prices weaken or external demand slows. As part of its long-term development strategy, the government is promoting investment in green energy, incentivizing solar and wind power projects in the hope of creating new export opportunities for electricity.

7. Nonetheless, concerns about structural unemployment remain. Skill mismatches persist between job seekers and the needs of modern industries, particularly in the technology sector. Rural-urban migration has become more common, pressuring housing and public services in Baku, while leaving some villages with labor shortages. Recognizing these challenges, the Ministry of Education has partnered with private companies to revamp vocational education programs, aiming to align training with rapidly evolving market demands.

Table 1: Selected Macroeconomic Indicators for Azerbaijan (2019—2022)

| Indicator                             | 2019 | 2020 | 2021 | 2022 |      |  |
|---------------------------------------|------|------|------|------|------|--|
| Nominal GDP (US$ billion)            |      | 47.0 | 42.5 | 45.8 | 52.0 |  |
| Real GDP Growth Rate (%)              |      | 2.2  | 2.2  | 3.4  | 4.6  |  |
| Inflation Rate (%)                    | 2.6  | 3.0  | 4.5  | 8.4  |      |  |
| Unemployment Rate (%)                 |      | 5.3  | 6.5  | 6.2  | 5.9  |  |
| Exchange Rate (AZN per US$)          |      | 1.70 | 1.70 | 1.70 | 1.69 |  |
| Govt. Capital Spending (US$ billion) | 0.9  | 1.4  | 2.2  | 2.4  |      |  |

Table 2: Trade and Social Indicators

| Indicator                                         | 2019 | 2021   | 2022   |
|---------------------------------------------------|------|--------|--------|
| Oil/Gas Exports as % of Total Exports             |      | 85%    | 85%    |
| Agricultural Tariff Rate (selected products)      |      | 0%     | 10%    |
| Gini Coefficient                                  | 0.33 | 0.34   | 0.35   |
| Avg. Monthly Household Subsidy (utilities & food) |      | US$40 | US$45 |
| FDI Inflows (US$ billion)                        | 4.2  | 3.7    | 4.0    |

parts.markscheme:
- Subtract 3600 from 3780 to get 180.  
- Divide 180 by 3600 and multiply by 100.  
- The answer is 5%.

parts.content: Using information from Table 2, calculate the price elasticity of demand (PED) for German cars when the average price increases from USD35,000 to USD40,000.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate percentage change in quantity demanded: ((2200 − 2500) ÷ 2500) × 100 = −12%.  
- Calculate percentage change in price: ((40,000 − 35,000) ÷ 35,000) × 100 ≈ 14.29%.  
- PED = −12% ÷ 14.29% ≈ −0.84.

parts.content: Using information from Table 3, calculate Germany’s unemployment rate for 2022.
parts.markscheme:
- Subtract 40.7 million from 43.0 million to get 2.3 million unemployed.  
- (2.3 ÷ 43.0) × 100 ≈ 5.35%.

parts.content: Using the additional information provided, calculate the Keynesian multiplier for Germany.
parts.markscheme:
- The marginal propensity to save (MPS) = 1 − MPC = 1 − 0.7 = 0.3.  
- Sum of leakages = MPS + MPT + MPM = 0.3 + 0.2 + 0.1 = 0.6.  
- Multiplier = 1 ÷ 0.6 ≈ 1.67.

parts.content: Define the term progressive tax.
parts.markscheme: A progressive tax is a tax where the proportion of income paid in tax rises as income increases.

parts.content: Using an AD/AS diagram, explain how a €50 billion increase in government spending might affect Germany’s level of real output.

- Additional government spending increases aggregate demand, shifting the AD curve to the right from AD1 to AD2.  
- Real output rises from Y to Yp and the price level may increase from P1 to P2 if the economy is near full capacity.  
- Higher government spending can have a multiplier effect, further boosting real GDP.  
- The extent of the increase in output depends on the level of spare capacity in the economy.

parts.content: Using information from Table 3, explain how a falling unemployment rate could affect consumption in Germany.
parts.markscheme:
- A lower unemployment rate means more people are employed and earning income, increasing consumption.  
- Higher disposable incomes raise aggregate demand, potentially leading to further growth in real GDP.  
- Increased employment can improve consumer confidence, encouraging even greater spending.  
- Greater spending shifts AD curve rightward (AD1 -> AD2), as its components of Real GDP. This increases Real output (Y -> Yp) and increases price level (pl1 -> pl2)
- More aggregate spending can create a positive feedback loop, contributing to further demand for goods and services.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and knowledge of economics, recommend a policy which the German government could implement in order to reduce income inequality in Germany.
parts.markscheme:
- Income inequality in Germany is relatively low (Gini coefficient of 0.29), yet significant wealth remains concentrated in the top decile; progressive taxes already reach 42% for high earners.  
- A policy that expands spending on vocational training and education could reduce structural unemployment, improve labor market opportunities, and narrow income gaps over time.  
- Increasing government funding for programs that upskill workers in the automotive and manufacturing sectors could target groups whose jobs are vulnerable to global supply chain disruptions, referencing the case study’s mention of vulnerabilities in labor markets.  
- Additional investments in infrastructure (as already budgeted at €50 billion) can be directed toward regions with lower income levels, supporting local job creation and balanced economic development.  
- This approach can increase long-term earning potential by giving workers more skills, which could reduce reliance on wealth concentrated in the top decile.  
- The diagram shows a rightward shift of the long-run aggregate supply curve from LRAS1 to LRAS2, increasing potential real output from Y1 to Y2, illustrating the long-term impact of increased government investment in education, vocational training, and infrastructure.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/15fe6104-00c8-4923-894b-f2e26f410537)

- Stronger public services, financed through progressive taxation, can enhance equal opportunities for lower-income groups, aligning with the existing tax structure’s aim of redistributing income.  
- One advantage is that focusing on human capital formation leads to higher productivity and sustainable economic growth, benefiting both the economy’s competitiveness and reducing inequality.  
- A potential drawback is that such programs require significant government expenditure and might impose higher opportunity costs or crowd out other spending.  
- The policy increases equity by improving earning potential for lower-income households while still preserving incentives for higher earners in the progressive tax system.  
- Overall, targeted education and training programs, coupled with progressive taxation, can reduce income disparity and support continued expansion of Germany’s strong manufacturing base.

questions.specification:
Germany is Europe’s largest economy, with a strong manufacturing base and a highly skilled workforce. In recent years, the German government has encouraged the transition to electric vehicles (EVs) to reduce carbon emissions and maintain its competitiveness in the global automobile market. Despite these efforts, inequality remains a concern, with a sizable gap between high and low-income earners.

The German government launched a €20 billion stimulus package in 2022 to support green technologies and sustain economic growth. Economists estimate that Germany’s marginal propensity to consume (MPC) is 0.8.

Table 1: Germany’s Electric Vehicle (EV) Market (2023)

| Price of a mid-range EV (euros) | Quantity Demanded (thousands of units) | Quantity Supplied (thousands of units) |
|---------------------------------|----------------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| 40 000                          | 600                                    | 550                                    |
| 38 000                          | 650                                    | 540                                    |

Table 2: Germany’s Real GDP (Selected Years)

| Year       | Real GDP (billion euros) |
|------------|--------------------------|
| 2020       | 3 367                    |
| 2021       | 3 572                    |
| 2022       | 3 870                    |
| 2023 (est) | 3 930                    |

Table 3: Income Distribution in Germany (2022)

| Indicator                               | Value  |
|----------------------------------------|--------|
| Gini coefficient                       | 0.31   |
| Income share held by top 10%           | 27%    |
| Income share held by bottom 10%        | 3%     |

Although Germany imposes a progressive income tax system (ranging from 14% to 45%, depending on income brackets), policymakers are still evaluating measures to make growth more inclusive. Rising consumption, spurred by stimulus and robust consumer confidence, has led to renewed concerns about demand-pull inflation. Furthermore, the euro’s exchange rate has recently appreciated, which could affect the competitiveness of German exports.

parts.markscheme:
• Lower-income earners with only 3% of income share may be unable to spend enough to sustain higher aggregate demand, limiting growth prospects.  
• Inequality can restrict access to education or training for individuals in lower-income brackets, reducing Germany’s long-term productivity gains and innovation potential.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and your knowledge of economics, recommend a policy that the German government could implement to address income inequality while maintaining economic growth.
parts.markscheme:
• Income inequality is the unequal distribution of income across individuals in an economy.  
• Economic growth is the increase in real output over time, as seen in Germany’s rising real GDP figures.  
• A progressive tax system charges a higher percentage of income tax on higher earners than on lower earners.  

• There could be a strengthened progressive tax reform that slightly raises the marginal rate on the highest income bracket to redistribute income from high to low earners, using Germany’s existing tax structure ranging from 14% to 45%.  
• A portion of the higher tax revenue could be directed to education and vocational training programs, improving workforce skills and helping low-income earners overcome barriers to job opportunities, thereby enhancing Germany’s skilled labor base.  
• The greater government spending on skill development could shift Germany’s aggregate supply outward over time by increasing labor productivity, supporting sustainable economic growth.  
• By raising disposable incomes for lower-income groups, domestic consumption would rise, boosting aggregate demand and contributing to short-term output growth.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/d97f6e9d-4466-4e1c-9877-3f72d61bee23)
• The measure could also moderate the 0.31 Gini coefficient if resources are redistributed effectively, allowing more inclusive participation in economic progress.  
• The policy addresses concerns about demand-pull inflation by focusing on longer-term productivity gains, which can keep costs in check rather than fueling price pressures solely through consumer spending.  
• With the euro’s appreciation, relying on stronger domestic demand can support overall GDP growth even if export competitiveness is challenged.  
• Allocating part of the stimulus package introduced in 2022 for further social support initiatives, rather than only green technology projects, can counter the 27% share of income held by the top 10% and improve inclusivity.  
• Ensuring that the benefits extend beyond short-term cash transfers can create more human capital accumulation among lower earners, which maintains growth momentum.  
• This balanced approach tackles income inequality through targeted government intervention while preserving the strong economic performance driven by both consumption and improvements in productivity.

parts.content: Using real-world examples, evaluate the effectiveness of tariffs in increasing domestic producers’ welfare.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:
### Definition:

- **Tariff**: A tax placed on imported goods, intended to increase the price of imported products and protect domestic industries from foreign competition.
- **Domestic Producers’ Welfare**: The overall well-being or gains to domestic producers, often measured through changes in producer surplus, profitability, and market share.

### Economic Theory:

- A tariff shifts the world supply curve upwards by the amount of the tax when imports enter the domestic market.
- Domestic producers can raise their prices closer to the artificially higher import price, as foreign competition decreases, potentially increasing producer surplus in the short run.
- By limiting foreign competition, tariffs can protect emerging or strategic domestic industries by reducing competitive pressure and allowing them to develop economies of scale. Which may allow them to benefit from economies of scale over time and expand production.
- In the presence of inelastic demand for the good, domestic producers can capture more revenue as consumers struggle to find substitutes, raising domestic producer welfare.
- However, tariffs tend to raise prices for consumers since the cost of imported goods increases, leading to a decrease in consumer surplus. They may also reduce competition in the domestic market, potentially decreasing efficiency and innovation in the long run.
- Tariffs can provoke retaliatory measures by trading partners, which may damage export markets and reduce overall economic welfare.
- Net welfare loss arises from the inefficient allocation of resources and reduced consumption, which can eventually hinder the long-term prospects of domestic producers if global competitive pressures are avoided for too long.

### Diagram:

- Microeconomic diagram showing a tariff on an import:
  - Global supply shifts up by the value of the tariff.
  - Domestic equilibrium price rises.
  - Domestic producers’ output and producer surplus increase in the short run.
  - Overall social welfare decreases due to the creation of a deadweight loss.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/845899bb-0d4c-4189-92d3-054a656a3658)

### Evaluation:

- Short-term impacts:

  - In the short term, tariffs can raise domestic producer welfare by increasing market share and output since foreign competitors face higher costs and reduce their supply, as foreign competitors face higher costs. This allows domestic firms to expand production and increase profits.

- Long-term impacts:

  - Over time, domestic producers may become complacent without foreign competition, as reduced pressure weakens incentives to innovate, affecting efficiency and innovation. Higher input costs and possible retaliatory tariffs on exports can erode gains by reducing export demand and increasing production costs.

### **Real World Examples**:

- In 2018, the United States imposed tariffs on steel (25%) and aluminum (10%). U.S. steelmakers initially reported higher profits and an uptick in production due to reduced foreign competition. However, downstream industries that rely on steel as an input faced higher costs, and some reported lower output.
- China responded with retaliatory tariffs on U.S. goods, including agricultural products. This hurt U.S. agricultural producers, showcasing how retaliation can indirectly reduce overall producer welfare.
- In 2013, the European Union imposed tariffs on Chinese solar panels. European panel manufacturers initially experienced higher sales volumes, but the costs for solar projects rose, slowing industry growth. Global competition declined, creating a less dynamic domestic market over time.

### Conclusion:

- Tariffs can raise domestic producers’ welfare (European Union solar panel tariffs or U.S steel and aluminum) in the short run by increasing producer surplus and protecting certain industries from foreign competition.
- Long-term sustainability is less certain, as higher domestic prices, lower efficiency, and retaliatory measures can undermine the net benefits to producers by increasing costs and reducing competitiveness. This can lead to declining output and profits over time.

questions.specification:
## Zambia’s Economic Transition: From Copper Dependency to Sustainable Development


Zambia is a landlocked country in south-central Africa with an estimated population of 19 million. Over the past decade, Zambia’s economy has experienced moderate growth driven largely by copper exports, which account for nearly 70% of the country’s total export earnings. However, frequent fluctuations in global commodity prices have resulted in volatile export revenues. In 2022, the Zambian kwacha depreciated by nearly 10%, trading at around ZMW18 per US dollar, raising concerns over inflation and the cost of imported goods.

The government has sought to promote economic diversification beyond copper by investing in agriculture, tourism, and small-scale manufacturing. Despite these efforts, poverty remains widespread: approximately 54% of the population lives below the national poverty line, with rural areas most severely affected. To tackle this, Zambia’s government increased social spending: in 2021, about 12% of its total budget was allocated to improving rural infrastructure, secondary education, and healthcare services. Policymakers hope that these measures will boost productivity and reduce inequality in the long run.

One persistent challenge is maintaining macroeconomic stability in the face of high external debt and fluctuating copper prices. Inflation has hovered between 15% and 20% annually in the past few years. The government implemented tighter monetary policies in late 2021, including higher reserve requirements for commercial banks, aiming to limit excess money creation and stabilize the exchange rate. In addition, Zambia entered negotiations with international financial institutions for debt restructuring, seeking to create more fiscal space for social programs.

On the microeconomic front, price floors and subsidies in the agricultural sector continue to spark debate. Maize, the country’s staple crop, has benefited from guaranteed minimum prices set by the Food Reserve Agency. Proponents argue this policy supports farmers’ incomes and food security, while critics fear inefficiencies and budgetary strains. Furthermore, access to credit remains a bottleneck for many small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs), preventing them from scaling up and creating more job opportunities.

Zambia also participates in regional trade agreements, such as the Common Market for Eastern and Southern Africa (COMESA). Tariff reductions within the region have encouraged some diversification in exports—particularly of agricultural goods and textiles to neighboring countries. Recently, the government signed a new bilateral trade deal with Malawi and Zimbabwe to reduce non-tariff barriers, with the idea of boosting cross-border trade. Officials hope this agreement will expand the market for Zambia’s emerging non-traditional exports, such as processed foods and manufactured goods, fostering greater resilience against future commodity price shocks.

Despite these positive developments, inequality and poverty remain pressing concerns. The government estimates that about 300,000 new entrants join the labor force annually, yet formal employment growth lags behind. Zambia’s human development indicators highlight the challenges: literacy rates in some rural provinces remain below 60%, while malnutrition affects nearly 35% of children under five. The government is therefore focusing on vocational training and improved healthcare to interrupt what development economists often refer to as the “poverty cycle,” wherein low incomes lead to low investment in human capital, perpetuating underdevelopment.

Private foreign direct investment (FDI) has recently targeted not only mining but also Zambia’s small manufacturing sector and retail services. However, uncertainty around regulatory changes and debt sustainability may temper investor confidence. If foreign investment in education and healthcare can be encouraged alongside initiatives to improve infrastructure, many economists believe Zambia has the potential to achieve more stable and inclusive growth.

Overall, Zambia’s policy mix aims to balance immediate social needs with sustainable growth. While copper exports will likely remain an important source of foreign exchange, reducing dependence on a single commodity is widely seen as essential. The combination of social programs, targeted subsidies, and deepening regional trade may help Zambia overcome enduring challenges related to poverty, volatility, and a narrow economic base.

**Table 1: Zambia’s Selected Macroeconomic Indicators (2019–2022)**

| Indicator                          | 2019     | 2020     | 2021     | 2022     |
|------------------------------------|----------|----------|----------|----------|
| Real GDP Growth Rate (%)           | 2.0      | -2.8     | 1.6      | 3.2      |
| Inflation Rate (%)                 | 12.5     | 17.5     | 19.0     | 16.3     |
| Unemployment Rate (%)              | 11.0     | 12.5     | 12.8     | 11.9     |
| Government Budget Balance (% GDP)  | -7.0     | -9.8     | -8.5     | -7.2     |
| Exchange Rate (ZMW per US Dollar)  | 14.0     | 17.0     | 18.2     | 18.0     |

**Table 2: Zambia’s Sectoral Export Composition (2022)**

| Sector                  | Share of Total Exports (%) |
|-------------------------|----------------------------|
| Copper and Minerals     | 68                         |
| Agricultural Products   | 15                         |
| Manufactured Goods      | 10                         |
| Textiles                | 4                          |
| Tourism Services        | 3                          |

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/0767f8b5-2e65-4860-8848-daabd3ed25c7)

Setting a minimum price (Pfloor) above the equilibrium for maize can lead to a surplus where the quantity supplied (Qs) exceeds the quantity demanded (Qd). Farmers benefit from higher prices (Pe -> Pfloor), which might increase their incomes. However, it can result in government budgetary pressures if authorities purchase the surplus or store it. It may also encourage inefficiencies if farmers rely heavily on guaranteed prices rather than improving productivity. The demand and supply diagram shows a new price floor above the original equilibrium price, creating excess supply in the maize market.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using a Lorenz curve diagram, explain the possible implications of Zambia’s poverty and high income inequality for its long-term growth potential. (paragraph 6)
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ccbc52ed-963f-484d-b878-1c978468c4f5)

Zambia’s extensive poverty and high income inequality can widen the gap in the Lorenz curve between perfect equality and actual income distribution, indicating a more unequal distribution of income (L1 -> L2). Over time, high inequality often reduces long-term growth by discouraging investment in human capital, limiting access to education and healthcare for lower-income groups, and diminishing overall productive capacity. The Lorenz curve diagram would show a more pronounced bow away from the line of equality, reflecting a large share of income accruing to relatively few individuals.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using a PPC (Production Possibility Curve) diagram, explain how improvements in education and healthcare (as mentioned in the text) might shift Zambia’s long-term production capacity. (paragraph 6)
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/96cdde79-9a11-41a6-857b-408cb4c97cf4)

Improvements in education and healthcare enhance human capital and labor productivity. On a PPC diagram, this shifts the entire curve outward in the long run (PPC1 -> PPC2), indicating that the economy can produce more of both capital and consumer goods. Better healthcare reduces absenteeism, while improved education boosts skills and innovation. Over time, these factors expand Zambia’s potential output and promote sustainable economic growth.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using a poverty cycle diagram, explain how low levels of income and investment in human capital can perpetuate poverty in Zambia. (paragraph 6)
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/5e125c1b-ce3d-4dc1-bbf7-7685162d36d7)

Low incomes limit access to quality education and healthcare, which in turn reduces human capital formation. This perpetuates a poverty cycle where households remain at subsistence levels, have minimal savings, and invest little in their children’s schooling. A poverty cycle diagram shows that underinvestment in human capital leads to low productivity, perpetuating low wages and continued poverty. Without policy intervention to break the cycle- such as expanded social spending and skill training - poverty tends to persist across generations.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using information from the text/data (including Table 2) and your knowledge of economics, discuss the potential impact of Zambia’s new trade agreements on economic growth and development.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 

**Definition:**
- Economic growth: an increase in real output and real incomes over time.  
- Economic development: improvements in standards of living, reduction in poverty, enhanced human capital, and greater economic security.  
- Trade agreement: an arrangement between countries that reduces trade barriers and fosters economic cooperation.  

**Range of Arguments**

- Diversification of Exports  
- Lower or eliminated tariffs within regional agreements may encourage producers to diversify away from copper and minerals (68% of exports in Table 2) toward agricultural products (15%) and manufactured goods (10%).  
- By shifting to new sectors such as processed foods and textiles, Zambia can reduce reliance on volatile copper prices, stabilizing export revenues and foreign exchange earnings.  
- However, limited infrastructure, credit constraints, and weak institutions could slow the pace of diversification if farmers and SMEs cannot scale up production to meet regional demand.


- Enhanced Market Access and Economies of Scale  
- Trade deals with Malawi and Zimbabwe reduce non-tariff barriers and permit easier entry into neighboring markets, potentially expanding the customer base for Zambian products.  
- Larger markets allow firms to achieve economies of scale, potentially lowering average costs and improving competitiveness.  
- Yet, the small size of consumer markets in neighboring countries might limit the full potential gains, and there is competition from other regional players.
- Higher export revenues increase Aggregate Demand of Zambia, as exports are component of GDP, causing a rightward shift of AD1 to AD2. This causes a hike in price level and higher Real GDP. 

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/78d3439f-e114-4741-8316-5142a5534513)

- Attraction of Foreign Direct Investment  
- Access to broader regional markets may attract FDI beyond the mining sector and into manufacturing or agro-processing, supporting technology transfer and local employment.  
- Stable macroeconomic policy and negotiated debt restructuring might boost investor confidence if Zambia demonstrates fiscal discipline.  
- Investors could be deterred by persistent concerns over debt sustainability and the potential for policy reversals, limiting the magnitude of FDI inflows.

- Potential Effects on Income and Employment  
- Growth in non-traditional export sectors could raise incomes for small-scale farmers and entrepreneurs, promoting more inclusive growth if accompanied by improved access to credit and training.  
- Long-term employment creation may help reduce the 300,000 new annual labor force entrants who struggle to find formal jobs.  
- If protective policies in favor of copper exports persist, the transition toward labor-intensive industries may proceed too slowly to absorb excess labor.

- Impacts on Government Revenue and Social Spending  
- Successful export-led growth could increase government tax revenue, thereby expanding fiscal space to invest in education, healthcare, and infrastructure.  
- Higher social spending could reduce the high poverty rate (54%) documented in rural areas over time by creating better social services and safety nets.  
- If commodity price shocks recur and export earnings remain unstable, the government may need to rely on external financing, risking debt sustainability and constraining development spending.

- Potential Regional Cooperation Benefits  
- Regional infrastructure projects, like joint transport corridors, could lower the cost of trading, benefiting Zambia’s landlocked position.  
- Sharing technology and best practices across COMESA members might speed up Zambia’s productivity growth, although differences in regulatory frameworks can slow progress.  
- Poor coordination and administrative bottlenecks can erode potential gains from these agreements if not consistently addressed by policymakers.

**Overall Evaluation** 
- Short-term gains may include higher export volumes, increased FDI inflows, and greater employment in diversified industries.  
- Long-term development depends on continued investment in human capital, improved infrastructure, and effective governance to ensure that trade benefits are broadly shared.  
- External risks, including global economic downturns and debt management challenges, may temper the positive effects of regional integration.

**Conclusion**  
- Zambia’s new trade agreements could foster broader economic growth if accompanied by policies that improve infrastructure, strengthen human capital, and ensure effective implementation of trade reforms. While diversified exports and regional cooperation can reduce dependence on copper, the country’s success in achieving sustainable development depends on proactively addressing factors such as poverty, inequality, and debt sustainability.

questions.specification:
Tajikistan is a mountainous, landlocked country in Central Asia. It faces several development challenges, including a relatively high rate of poverty, a narrow export base (primarily cotton and aluminum), an aging infrastructure, and a heavy reliance on worker remittances sent from abroad—remittances constitute about 28% of the country’s GDP by some estimates. The agricultural sector accounts for about 20% of GDP but employs around 45% of the labour force. While the country has achieved steady growth in recent years, much of the population remains vulnerable to external shocks such as declining global commodity prices, extreme weather events, and reductions in remittances.

The government’s revenue is primarily derived from three main types of taxes: corporate income tax, personal income tax, and a value-added tax (VAT). Despite the relatively low nominal rates, challenges in tax collection remain. Tajikistan’s trade balance has been in persistent deficit for most of the last decade, with imports of consumer and capital goods outstripping exports such as cotton, aluminum, and fresh fruits. The country’s real GDP growth, unemployment rate, and Gini coefficient are presented in Table 1.

Below is a snapshot of key macroeconomic indicators, the country’s tax structure, selected data on income distribution, and information on cotton as a key export.

**Table 1: Macroeconomic Indicators in Tajikistan (2017–2021)**

| Year | Real GDP (TJS billions) | Real GDP Growth Rate (%) | Gini Coefficient | Unemployment Rate (%) |
|------|-------------------------|--------------------------|------------------|-----------------------|
| 2017 | 63                      | 7.0                      | 0.34             | 11.4                  |
| 2018 | 67                      | 6.3                      | 0.34             | 11.1                  |
| 2019 | 71                      | 6.9                      | 0.35             | 10.8                  |
| 2020 | 77                      | 8.5                      | 0.36             | 10.6                  |
| 2021 | 82                      | 6.5                      | 0.37             | 10.3                  |

**Table 2: Tax Structure in Tajikistan**

| Type of Tax           | Rate of Tax                                |
|-----------------------|--------------------------------------------|
| Corporate Income Tax  | 23%                                        |
| Personal Income Tax   | Progressive rate up to 25%                 |
| Value-Added Tax (VAT) | 18% on most goods and services (some 0%)   |

**Table 3: Income Distribution by Population Groups (2021)**

| Population Group          | Share of Total Income (%) |
|---------------------------|---------------------------|
| Top 20%                   | 45                        |
| Middle 40%                | 35                        |
| Bottom 40%                | 20                        |

Figure 1: Market for Cotton in Tajikistan (2021 → 2022)

Assume the price of cotton per bale (in TJS) increased from 10 to 12 between 2021 and 2022. Over the same period, the quantity demanded decreased from 500 000 bales to 470 000 bales, while the quantity supplied increased from 400 000 bales to 420 000 bales.

In 2021, the government introduced a stimulus package injecting TJS 600 million into the economy (for infrastructure projects and social spending). Economists estimate the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) in Tajikistan to be about 0.75.

Tajikistan’s heavy reliance on foreign remittances has raised concerns about macroeconomic vulnerability. A sudden drop in remittance inflows could constrain households’ consumption, reduce revenue from indirect taxes, and undermine the country’s balance of payments.

parts.content: Calculate the percentage change in real GDP from 2020 to 2021.
parts.markscheme:
- (Percentage change in real GDP from 2020 to 2021)  
- (82 – 77) ÷ 77 × 100 = 5 ÷ 77 × 100 ≈ 6.5%

parts.content: Using the data in Table 3, calculate the ratio of the top 20% share of income to the bottom 40% share of income in 2021. Show your workings.
parts.markscheme:
- Ratio of top 20% share to bottom 40% share)  
- Ratio = 45% ÷ 20% = 2.25 → 2.25:1

parts.content: Using data from Figure 1, calculate the price elasticity of demand (PED) for cotton when the price changes from TJS 10 to TJS 12 per bale. Provide the absolute value of the PED in your answer.
parts.markscheme:
- (PED for cotton from TJS 10 to TJS 12)  
  % change in QD = [(470 000 – 500 000) ÷ 500 000] × 100 = –6%  
 - % change in price = [(12 – 10) ÷ 10] × 100 = 20%  
 - PED = –6% ÷ 20% = –0.3 → Absolute value = 0.3

parts.content: Using data from Figure 1, calculate the change in total revenue for cotton producers in Tajikistan following the increase in price from TJS 10 to TJS 12 per bale.
parts.markscheme:
- Change in total revenue for cotton producers)  
- Initial total revenue = 10 × 500 000 = TJS 5 000 000  
- Final total revenue = 12 × 470 000 = TJS 5 640 000  
- Change in total revenue = 5 640 000 – 5 000 000 = TJS 640 000

parts.content: Define the term “Keynesian multiplier.”
parts.markscheme: The Keynesian multiplier is the factor by which an initial injection into the economy leads to a proportionately larger change in national income.

parts.content: Using an appropriate AD/AS diagram, explain how an injection of TJS 600 million into the economy (with an MPC of 0.75) might affect Tajikistan’s real GDP.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/d0a3440c-3676-49db-8478-dd5f0a63699b)

- An injection of TJS 600 million with an MPC of 0.75 implies a multiplier of 1 ÷ (1 – 0.75) = 4, creating a multiplied effect on aggregate demand.  
- In an AD/AS diagram, AD shifts to the right from AD1 to AD2, leading to higher real GDP (Y -> Yp).  
- The rise in real GDP is proportionally larger than the initial injection due to the high MPC.  - The new equilibrium shows increased output and potentially slightly higher average price levels (pl1 -> pl2), assuming short-run aggregate supply is upward-sloping.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using the information provided, calculate the total value of Tajikistan’s stimulus package as a percentage of its real GDP in 2021.
parts.markscheme:
- Real GDP in 2021 = TJS 82 billion
- Stimulus package = TJS 600 million = TJS 0.6 billion

$$
\text{Stimulus as \% of GDP} = \left( \frac{0.6}{82} \right) \times 100 = 0.7317\%
$$

Answer: Approximately 0.73%

parts.content: Using information from the text, explain two ways in which reliance on remittances may act as a barrier to economic development in Tajikistan.
parts.markscheme:
- Reliance makes the economy vulnerable to external shocks if remittances suddenly drop, reducing consumption and government tax revenue.  
- Dependence on external income can reduce incentives to diversify the domestic economy, limiting opportunities to develop local industries and undermining long-term growth.

parts.content: Using the information in the text and your knowledge of economics, evaluate the extent to which reliance on remittances is a barrier to economic development in Tajikistan.
parts.markscheme:
- Economic growth: An increase in a country’s real output (real GDP) over time.  
- Sustainable growth: Growth that meets present needs without compromising future resources or welfare.  
- Remittances: Transfers of money from citizens working abroad back to individuals in the home country.

- Tajikistan’s reliance on remittances at about 28% of GDP and its narrow export base create vulnerability to global economic changes and make households dependent on external income. Persistent trade deficits and a high share of consumer and capital goods imports signal the need to foster domestic production capacity.

- Strategies to reduce reliance on remittances typically revolve around supply-side policies, improving infrastructure, and diversifying exports. - This would shift the long-run aggregate supply curve outward, reducing unemployment and increasing potential output. Stronger industries at home can boost exports, contribute to more balanced trade, and provide stable sources of income for workers.

- Investment in domestic industries and infrastructure increases productive capacity, shifting LRAS right from LRAS1 to LRAS2. 
- This shows long-term economic growth without causing inflation, as output rises from Y to Yp. The diagram reflects how supply-side policies reduce reliance on remittances by expanding sustainable domestic production and exports.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/a713e81a-edc3-49e0-8aeb-301e880722de)

- Introduce targeted investment incentives for domestic industries, particularly in sectors beyond cotton and aluminum (for example, small-scale manufacturing or value-added agricultural products), to expand the narrow export base, encourage entrepreneurship, and improve productivity.  
- Grants or tax breaks can incentivize businesses to invest in modern equipment and technology, helping producers move up the value chain.
- Improved infrastructure, financed partly through tax revenues, can connect rural agricultural areas to urban markets, raising incomes and reducing the incentive to seek work abroad.

- Refer to the case study while explaining how the policy addresses issues:  
Such a policy leverages existing agricultural strengths (20% of GDP) by modernizing farming methods to ensure stable domestic production and reduce vulnerability to fluctuations in remittances.  
- Better infrastructure aids transportation for exports like cotton and fresh fruits, while also supporting diversification into new manufacturing or service sectors that can generate employment and tax revenues.

- In the short term, the policy might require significant government spending and sustained efforts in tax collection (a challenge mentioned in the text), implying potential opportunity costs for the government.  
- In the long term, successful diversification can reduce poverty rates, protect incomes from global commodity shocks, and increase stability in the balance of payments. This creates more sustainable growth by boosting domestic production, reducing vulnerability to remittance flows, and raising overall living standards.

- The recommended supply-side policy of incentivizing new industries and investing in infrastructure addresses the reliance on foreign remittances by creating additional sources of income, strengthens Tajikistan’s export potential, and promotes more sustainable economic growth.

questions.specification:
The United Kingdom (UK) is one of the largest economies in Europe, with its gross domestic product (GDP) predominantly driven by services, manufacturing, and financial activities. In 2021, the UK economy showed signs of recovery from the global downturn of 2020, despite ongoing uncertainties related to international trade and supply chain disruptions. The government remains concerned about persistent income inequality, slow productivity growth, and a continuing current account deficit.

Due to strong demand and limited housing supply, average house prices in the UK have risen sharply in recent years, prompting concerns of an affordability crisis. Economists estimate that the price elasticity of supply (PES) for new homes is relatively low (0.60 in the short run), while the demand for houses tends to be price inelastic. The UK government has also introduced various fiscal policies to stimulate growth. In 2022, an infrastructure spending package of £20 billion was announced, with some analysts estimating a marginal propensity to consume (MPC) of 0.8, implying a potentially significant multiplier effect on aggregate demand.

Below are selected data on macroeconomic indicators, the housing market, and the UK taxation system.

Table 1: UK Macroeconomic Indicators (2020–2022)

| Year | Real GDP (in £ billions) | Real GDP Growth Rate (%) | Government Spending (in £ billions) | Net Exports (in £ billions) | Gini Coefficient |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 2020 | 2000  | -9.9 | 350  | -20 | 0.34 |
| 2021 | 2160  | 8.0  | 370  | -30 | 0.35 |
| 2022 | 2270  | 5.1  | 390  | -25 | 0.35 |

Table 2: UK Housing Market Data (2021)

| Quarter | Average House Price (£) | Number of Houses Sold (000s) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Q1  | 270 000  | 290 |
| Q2  | 280 000  | 295 |
| Q3  | 310 000  | 285 |
| Q4  | 320 000  | 280 |

Table 3: Summary of UK Personal Income Tax Rates (2022)

| Income bracket (£ per year) | Income tax rate (%) |
| --- | --- |
| 0 – 12 570       | 0  |
| 12 570 – 50 270  | 20 |
| 50 271 – 150 000 | 40 |
| Over 150 000     | 45 |

Additionally, value-added tax (VAT) in the UK is charged at 20% on the majority of goods and services. In June 2022, a construction firm purchased new machinery for £120 000 (plus VAT) to increase its capacity for building new homes. 

Despite substantial exports of financial services and manufactured goods, the UK continues to run a negative net export figure. Some policymakers worry that an ongoing current account deficit could hinder long-term economic performance, while many economists are also focused on reducing the Gini coefficient of 0.35, considered moderate but persistent in the UK context.

parts.content: Explain why a persistent current account deficit might be considered a macroeconomic concern for the UK.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/0e0262eb-e88e-414d-9c22-6d201146cc4b)
A persistent current account deficit indicates that imports consistently exceed exports, leading to potential borrowing from abroad. It exerts downward pressure on the domestic currency while increasing foreign debt obligations. There is a risk of eroding confidence among international investors and creating vulnerabilities should foreign financing slow or become more expensive. It may also reduce long-term growth if debt servicing diverts resources from domestic investment.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using the information in the text, explain how the Keynesian multiplier could impact the level of real GDP following the £20 billion government spending initiative.
parts.markscheme: The Keynesian multiplier suggests that an initial government spending injection leads to multiple rounds of new spending. With an MPC of 0.8, the multiplier amplifies the initial £20 billion, resulting in a larger increase in aggregate demand than the initial injection. This additional spending circulates through the economy, raising consumption, output, and employment. The overall effect on real GDP is greater than the original spending due to repeated expenditure cycles.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and your knowledge of economics, recommend a policy which the UK government could implement to address the persistent current account deficit.
parts.markscheme:
• A policy that reduces the persistent current account deficit could involve an expenditure-switching approach, for example, a targeted export subsidy designed to make domestic exports more price-competitive in global markets.  
• A current account deficit occurs when the value of imports exceeds the value of exports for an extended period, which is reflected in the negative net export values in Table 1.  
• Export subsidies could lower producers’ costs, encouraging more production for overseas markets, thus boosting export revenue.  
• Greater export competitiveness could address the UK’s negative net export figure (−20 in 2020, −30 in 2021, −25 in 2022), helping to close the deficit by switching demand away from imports.  
• This policy could also mitigate some of the concerns that a persistent deficit raises, such as mounting foreign debt or downward pressure on the currency.  
• Expanding exports has the potential to strengthen aggregate demand, improving real GDP and reducing unemployment, supported by the UK’s strong services and manufacturing sectors.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/8674cad6-80a6-48e1-b87f-dc782c9374e9)
• The multiplier effect may enhance the benefits of growing exports if additional export revenue circulates domestically, stimulating consumption and investment.  
• However, there is a risk of retaliation from trading partners if subsidies are perceived as anti-competitive, which could lead to trade disputes and reduce overall gains in the long run.  
• The government could moderate this risk by ensuring that subsidies comply with international trade rules and by combining them with broader supply-side policies that improve productivity and long-term competitiveness.  
• Implementing this policy addresses the case study’s concern over the current account deficit by reinforcing the UK’s export base and moving the external balance closer to equilibrium while also enhancing economic performance.

questions.specification:
Germany is Europe’s largest economy and one of the world’s leading exporters. In 2022, Germany’s total exports, mainly in machinery and automobiles, contributed significantly to its economic growth. However, the economy faces challenges such as rising energy costs, concerns about income inequality, and the need to fulfill environmental targets. The German government maintains a progressive tax system and actively uses fiscal policy to stabilize economic fluctuations.

Economic Data  
Table 1 shows selected macroeconomic indicators for Germany in 2021 and 2022:

Table 1: Selected macroeconomic data for Germany (2021–2022)

| Indicator                          | 2021           | 2022           |
|------------------------------------|----------------|----------------|
| Real GDP (billion euros)           | 3,700          | 3,810          |
| Unemployment rate (%)              | 3.7            | 3.5            |
| Inflation rate (%)                 | 3.1            | 7.9            |
| Gini coefficient                   | 0.29           | 0.31           |

Recent government data suggest that Germany’s marginal propensity to consume (MPC) remains at approximately 0.8, prompting discussions about the potential impact of government spending on aggregate demand through the Keynesian multiplier.

Automobile Market  
Germany’s automobile industry is a vital component of its economy. Domestic demand for passenger cars is sensitive to price changes, especially with rising competition from electric vehicles. Table 2 presents simplified data for the German passenger car market:

Table 2: Car market data in Germany

| Price per car (euros) | Quantity demanded (millions of cars/year) |
|-----------------------|-------------------------------------------|
| 25,000                | 10.0                                      |
| 27,000                | 9.2                                       |

Tax System  
Germany adopts a progressive personal income tax system, with higher tax rates levied on higher income brackets. Table 3 provides examples of tax rates on personal income in Germany:

Table 3: Selected personal income tax rates in Germany

| Annual taxable income bracket (euros) | Marginal tax rate (%) |
|--------------------------------------|-----------------------|
| 0 – 9,984                             | 0                     |
| 9,985 – 58,596                        | 14 – 42               |
| Above 58,596                          | 42 – 45               |

According to the German Ministry of Finance, these progressive tax rates aim to redistribute income and reduce inequalities. However, debates continue regarding the effectiveness of tax policy in narrowing income disparities.

Environmental Concerns  
Germany’s car manufacturing sector faces pressure to reduce carbon emissions, which can impose negative externalities on society in the form of air pollution. Energy price increases have also affected production costs, potentially shifting supply and influencing equilibrium outcomes in domestic markets.

parts.content: Using information from Table 1, calculate the approximate real GDP growth rate for Germany between 2021 and 2022.
parts.markscheme:
• Subtract 2021 GDP from 2022 GDP: 3,810 – 3,700 = 110  
• Divide by 2021 GDP and multiply by 100: (110 / 3,700) × 100 ≈ 2.97%  
• Final answer: approximately 3.0%

parts.content: The German government plans to increase its spending by 20 billion euros. Assuming the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) is 0.8, calculate the total potential increase in real GDP that may result from this spending, using the Keynesian multiplier. Show your working.
parts.markscheme:
• Calculate the Keynesian multiplier as 1 / (1 – MPC) = 1 / (1 – 0.8) = 1 / 0.2 = 5  
• Multiply the government spending by the multiplier: 20 billion euros × 5 = 100 billion euros  
• Final answer: 100 billion euros

parts.content: Using the data in Table 2, calculate the price elasticity of demand for passenger cars in Germany when the price increases from 25,000 euros to 27,000 euros.
parts.markscheme:
• Percentage change in quantity demanded: ((9.2 – 10.0) / 10.0) × 100 = –8%  
• Percentage change in price: ((27,000 – 25,000) / 25,000) × 100 = +8%  
• Price elasticity of demand: (–8%) / 8% = –1.0

parts.content: Using the data in Table 2, calculate the change in total revenue for manufacturers when the price increases from 25,000 euros to 27,000 euros.
parts.markscheme:
• Original total revenue: 25,000 × 10.0 million = 250,000 million euros  
• New total revenue: 27,000 × 9.2 million = 248,400 million euros  
• Change in total revenue: 248,400 million – 250,000 million = –1,600 million euros

parts.content: Define the term “progressive tax.”
parts.markscheme: • A progressive tax is one where the proportion of income paid as tax rises as income increases.

parts.content: Using a supply and demand diagram, explain how rising energy costs for German car producers might affect the equilibrium price and quantity of cars.

• This leads to a higher equilibrium price and a lower equilibrium quantity in the market for cars.

parts.content: Using information from the text and Table 3, explain how Germany’s progressive tax system might influence income inequality.
parts.markscheme:
• Germany’s progressive income tax system ensures that higher-income earners pay a larger percentage of their income in taxes, which can help reduce income inequality.  
• Table 3 shows that the marginal tax rate increases with taxable income, aiming to narrow the gap between different income brackets.  
• The Gini coefficient data rose from 0.29 to 0.31, suggesting some increase in inequality, though the progressive system attempts to counteract this trend by redistributing income.  
• The text indicates that debates continue about the extent to which these tax rates effectively reduce disparities, but they are designed to lessen the income gap.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and your knowledge of economics, recommend a policy which the German government could implement to reduce negative externalities arising from carbon emissions in the car manufacturing sector. Justify your recommendation.
parts.markscheme:
- Negative externalities refer to costs imposed on third parties when consuming or producing a good, such as pollution from car manufacturing.  
- Carbon emissions create external costs not borne fully by car manufacturers or consumers, causing market failure.  
- A suitable policy to reduce these externalities could be a carbon tax. This tax would increase production costs for higher-emission vehicles, incentivizing firms to invest in cleaner technology.  
- The text highlights environmental concerns in Germany’s car manufacturing sector, where carbon emissions and rising energy prices are key issues. A carbon tax would directly address the pollution while encouraging more efficient production methods mentioned in the text.  
- Higher production costs for emission-intensive cars would shift the supply curve in a manner that reflects the true social cost of pollution, reducing output of high-emission vehicles.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/b0304fa7-7ab7-4a69-b506-fb1d71ab8fc3)

- The government could use this tax revenue to subsidize electric vehicle research and development, lowering the long-term cost of cleaner cars and reducing the impact of negative externalities.  
- This policy is within the syllabus (government intervention to address externalities) and targets the root cause of the problem: emissions that harm social welfare.  
- A potential drawback is increased production costs for manufacturers and higher prices for consumers, but the overall societal benefit from reduced pollution can justify the measure.  
- The policy aligns with the case study’s emphasis on environmental targets and would help fulfill Germany’s broader objective to lower carbon emissions.  
- The combination of a carbon tax and reinvestment of funds into cleaner energy sources and technologies could achieve a more sustainable and equitable outcome in car manufacturing.

questions.specification:
Benin is a West African country with a population of approximately 12.1 million people. The country’s economy relies heavily on agriculture, primarily the production and export of cotton. Benin’s government has recently focused on infrastructure investment to encourage economic growth and alleviate poverty. However, income inequality remains high, impeding further development efforts.

In 2021, Benin’s nominal GDP was US\$15 000 million with a GDP deflator of 120 (base year = 100). In 2022, nominal GDP increased to US\$16 800 million and the GDP deflator rose to 126. Over the same period, the government invested US$200 million in its “Roads and Education Project,” hoping to boost real GDP and improve human capital.

The government’s tax regime includes several tax brackets for personal income, up to a maximum marginal tax rate of 30%. Corporate taxes are set at 25%. Despite these efforts, the system faces challenges in its tax collection efficiency.

Table 1: Macroeconomic Indicators for Benin (2021–2022)  
| Year | Nominal GDP (US$ million) | GDP Deflator (base=100) | Government Infrastructure Spending (US$ million) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 2021 | 15 000 | 120 | 100 |
| 2022 | 16 800 | 126 | 200 |

Table 2: Benin’s Income Distribution Data (2022)  
| Population (millions) | Gini Coefficient | Poverty Rate (%) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 12.1 | 0.47 | 38 |

Table 3: Demand for Cotton in Benin  
| Price (US$/tonne) | Quantity Demanded (tonnes/month) |
| --- | --- |
| 350 | 22 000 |
| 400 | 20 000 |

Recent research suggests that the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) in Benin averages around 0.8. The government hopes that increased spending on infrastructure will boost aggregate demand, leading to higher employment and output. However, there are concerns that high levels of income inequality limit potential long-term development.

parts.content: Using the information provided in Table 1, calculate the real rate of GDP growth for Benin between 2021 and 2022.
parts.markscheme:
• Real GDP in 2021 = (Nominal GDP) ÷ (GDP Deflator/100) = 15 000 ÷ (120/100) = US$12 500 million  
• Real GDP in 2022 = 16 800 ÷ (126/100) = US$13 333 million (approximately)  
• Real rate of GDP growth (%) = [(13 333 – 12 500) ÷ 12 500] × 100 ≈ 6.67%

parts.content: Assuming that the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) in Benin is 0.8, calculate the potential total change in real GDP resulting from the government’s additional US\$100 million increase in infrastructure spending between 2021 and 2022 (that is, from US\$100 million to US\$200 million).
parts.markscheme:
• Multiplier = 1 ÷ (1 – MPC) = 1 ÷ (1 – 0.8) = 1 ÷ 0.2 = 5  
• Increase in government spending = US$100 million  
• Potential total change in real GDP = Multiplier × Increase in government spending = 5 × 100 million = US$500 million

parts.content: Using information from Table 3, calculate the price elasticity of demand (PED) for cotton when the price increases from US$350 per tonne to US$400 per tonne.
Using your answer to part (iii), calculate the percentage change in total revenue for cotton producers when the price increases from US$350 to US$400.
parts.markscheme:
• Initial total revenue = Price × Quantity = US$350 × 22 000 = US$7 700 000  
• New total revenue = US$400 × 20 000 = US$8 000 000  
• Percentage change in total revenue = [(8 000 000 – 7 700 000) ÷ 7 700 000] × 100 ≈ 3.90% increase

parts.content: Define the term progressive tax.
parts.markscheme: • A progressive tax is a tax system in which the proportion of income paid in tax rises as income increases.

parts.content: Using an AD/AS diagram, explain how an increase in government infrastructure spending (as shown in Table 1) might affect Benin’s real output.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/04e08c9b-8af7-4d27-a4fc-27f86f6c2cca)
• An increase in government infrastructure spending raises aggregate demand (AD) due to a direct increase in government expenditure.  
• This shifts the AD curve to the right in an AD/AS diagram (from AD1 to AD2).  
• Higher AD leads to higher real output (from Y1 to Y2) and potentially a higher price level.  
• Real output increases if there is spare capacity and resources in the economy; the extent depends on the shape of the aggregate supply curve.

parts.content: Using the information from Table 2, explain why high income inequality might act as a barrier to economic development in Benin.
parts.markscheme:
• High income inequality reduces the ability of lower-income groups to invest in education and health, as indicated by the 38% poverty rate in Table 2.  
• Limited spending power in large segments of the population restricts domestic demand, slowing growth.  
• Inequality can discourage entrepreneurial activity and human capital formation, constraining long-term development.  
• Benin’s Gini coefficient of 0.47 indicates a relatively unequal distribution of income, leading to structural barriers that limit broader economic development.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and your knowledge of economics, recommend a policy which could be implemented by the government of Benin to address the problem of high income inequality.
parts.markscheme:
• Income inequality is significant in Benin, as indicated by the 0.47 Gini coefficient and a 38% poverty rate, suggesting that many households lack sufficient resources.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/1b60690a-4e6e-4993-90be-01875ed45151)
• A possible government policy to reduce income inequality is to introduce or strengthen transfer payments aimed at low-income households, funded partially by progressive income taxes already in place.  
• Transfer payments provide direct financial support that can increase the purchasing power of poorer households and stimulate investment in education and health, aligning with infrastructure projects highlighted in the text.  
• The theory of income redistribution explains that channeling resources toward lower-income groups can reduce inequality and raise overall welfare, as those groups are more likely to spend additional income (high MPC), thereby boosting aggregate demand.  
• Implementing or increasing transfer payments can address long-term development by improving human capital and productivity, which aligns with the government’s goal of raising real GDP via infrastructure spending.  
• Referring to the text, stronger transfer payments could complement the “Roads and Education Project” by ensuring that poor populations can benefit more fully from new infrastructure and educational opportunities.  
• This policy can encourage a virtuous cycle: higher income for the poor increases demand, supports local businesses, and potentially reduces reliance on inefficient tax measures.  
• Evaluating potential drawbacks, the government must manage opportunity costs and ensure taxes are collected effectively to finance transfers without excessive budget deficits.  
• The MPC of 0.8 suggests that transfer payments would produce a strong multiplier effect, stimulating further growth but requiring careful monitoring to avoid inflationary pressures.  
• The recommendation is to strengthen targeted transfer payments as a complement to government infrastructure investment, reducing income inequality and fostering broader-based economic growth over time.

questions.specification:
Ghana’s economy has experienced relatively high levels of economic growth over the past decade, largely driven by the export of primary commodities, such as cocoa and gold, and an expanding services sector. Ghana is one of the world’s largest exporters of cocoa, and cocoa alone contributes a significant share of the country’s export earnings. Despite these gains, Ghana has faced macroeconomic challenges in recent years, including rising inflation and fiscal deficits.

The government has attempted to diversify the economy away from its reliance on primary commodities, investing in infrastructure and seeking to encourage more value-added activities to boost productivity and employment. However, issues such as income inequality and limited access to finance in rural areas persist and may limit the potential for inclusive growth.

Below are some data tables about Ghana’s economy.

Table 1: Selected Macroeconomic Indicators for Ghana (2022)

| Indicator                                              | Value                |
|--------------------------------------------------------|----------------------|
| Nominal GDP (US$ billions)                             | 74                   |
| Real GDP growth rate (%)                               | 3.7                  |
| Inflation rate (%) (year-on-year, end of period)       | 31                   |
| Government expenditure (GH¢ billions)                  | 103                  |
| Population (millions)                                  | 31.4                 |
| Gini coefficient                                       | 0.43                 |

Table 2: Cocoa Market Data (2022 estimates)

| Item                       | Value                |
|----------------------------|----------------------|
| Domestic demand for cocoa (tonnes)          | 70 000              |
| Domestic cocoa price (GH¢ per tonne)        | 10 000              |
| World cocoa price (GH¢ per tonne)           | 11 000              |
| Quantity exported (tonnes)                  | 800 000             |
| Estimated price elasticity of demand (PED)  | –0.5                |
| Estimated price elasticity of supply (PES)  | +0.3                |

Table 3: Income Tax Rates in Ghana

| Tax Rate Bracket            | Rate of Tax |
|-----------------------------|-------------|
| 0 – GH¢ 3 828/year         | 0 %         |
| GH¢ 3 828 – GH¢ 20 000/year | 15 %        |
| GH¢ 20 000 – GH¢ 40 000/year | 25 %        |
| Above GH¢ 40 000/year       | 30 %        |

Additional information:
• The marginal propensity to consume (MPC) in Ghana is estimated at 0.8.  
• Government tax revenue from cocoa exports is calculated as 10 % of the value of cocoa exported.  
• The government aims to reduce inflation by tightening monetary policy and improving the efficiency of its tax collection system.

Using the information in Table 1, calculate Ghana’s approximate GDP per capita in US dollars. Show your working.

From Table 2, using the estimated PES (+0.3), calculate the percentage change in quantity supplied of cocoa if its price rises from GH¢ 10 000 per tonne to GH¢ 11 000 per tonne in the domestic market.

parts.markscheme: • Income inequality is the uneven distribution of income among individuals or households within an economy.

parts.content: Using an AD/AS diagram, explain how tightening monetary policy could help reduce inflation in Ghana.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/c9323df4-9db3-4a30-a780-61e95e804b46)
• Tighter monetary policy raises interest rates, which reduces borrowing and spending in the economy, shifting the aggregate demand curve left.  
• Lower aggregate demand suppresses upward pressure on the general price level, helping to control inflation.  
• In the AD/AS diagram, this appears as a leftward shift of AD from AD1 to AD2, reducing the price level from PL1 to PL2 and lowering real output from Y1 to Y2.  
• This decrease in demand-pull pressure can help stabilize prices and contain high inflation.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>
