- Domestic cocoa price: GH¢ 10,000 per tonne
- Total revenue = Price × Quantity = 10,000 × 70,000
- Total revenue = GH¢ 700,000,000 (or GH¢ 700 million)

parts.content: With reference to the data in Table 1 and Table 3, explain two ways in which income tax policy could influence income distribution in Ghana.
parts.markscheme:
• A progressive tax structure, indicated by rising rates (15%, 25%, 30%) for higher brackets, could reduce income inequality by transferring a larger share of wealth from higher-income groups to the government, which could fund social programs.  
• Low or zero tax for lower-income earners (0% for up to GH¢ 3 828) helps maintain disposable income for the poorest, potentially reducing the Gini coefficient (0.43) through more equitable income distribution.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and your knowledge of economics, recommend a policy that could be implemented by the government of Ghana to promote inclusive growth (i.e., reduce income inequality while sustaining economic growth). Justify your recommendation.
parts.markscheme:
• Inclusive growth means economic expansion that provides opportunities and benefits across all socio-economic groups.  
• Income inequality refers to the uneven distribution of income, measured here by a Gini coefficient of 0.43, indicating moderate inequality.  
• Economic growth is the increase in real GDP, here achieved mainly through cocoa exports, gold, and a growing services sector.  
• A policy promoting vocational training and education subsidies in rural areas can reduce income inequality by enhancing skills and productivity, especially since limited access to finance and infrastructure constrains many rural communities.  
• This policy can potentially boost long-term aggregate supply (shifting LRAS right), sustaining growth without large inflationary pressures if managed carefully with ongoing investments in infrastructure.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/97a75aa6-7713-45b6-ae2c-e4cf5fcd5206)
• Financing for this policy can be drawn from improved tax collection and the government’s attempt to diversify the economy, as indicated by its focus on value-added activities and more efficient tax receipts.  
• Data on fiscal deficits (with government expenditure at GH¢ 103 billion) suggests a need for careful budgeting; however, spending on human capital is likely to yield returns by raising productivity and reducing reliance on volatile primary commodity exports.  
• Higher-skilled labor improves competitiveness in value-added sectors, enhancing exported services and products beyond raw cocoa or gold, thus supporting longer-term growth.  
• More equal opportunities reduce the gap between urban and rural incomes, improving inclusive growth and addressing persistent inequality, as reflected by the Gini coefficient.  
• This strategically balances the government’s goals: raising living standards, reducing inflationary pressures through a stronger productive base, and fostering a more equitable distribution of income.

questions.specification:
Greece, located in southeastern Europe, experienced significant economic challenges following the global financial crisis, resulting in high unemployment and repeated economic recessions. Despite recent reforms, in 2021 Greece’s unemployment rate remained one of the highest in the European Union, and concerns over income inequality persist. The government has attempted to implement fiscal and structural reforms aimed at achieving sustainable economic growth.  
Tourism, vital to the Greek economy, contributes nearly 20% of GDP and employs a substantial segment of the labor force. Additionally, shipping services remain a cornerstone of Greece’s export sector, generating around 25% of Greece’s export revenues. Another major export is olive oil, with demand both domestically and internationally.  
Greece’s progressive income tax system has marginal rates of up to 44%. The corporate income tax rate stands at 24%, and most goods and services are subject to a 24% value-added tax (VAT). In 2021, the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) was estimated at 0.75.

Table 1: Market Data for Greek Olive Oil  
| Year | Price (€/litre) | Quantity Demanded (million litres) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 2021 | 3.00 | 200 |
| 2022 | 3.60 | 180 |

Table 2: Key Macroeconomic Indicators for Greece (2019–2021)  
| Year | Real GDP (billion € at 2015 prices) | Annual Growth Rate (%) | Gini Coefficient | Unemployment Rate (%) | Government Spending (billion €) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 2019 | 175 | 1.9  | 0.331 | 17.3 | 47 |
| 2020 | 160 | -8.6 | 0.335 | 19.6 | 52 |
| 2021 | 174 |  8.8 | 0.337 | 16.3 | 51 |

parts.content: Using information from Table 1, calculate the price elasticity of demand (PED) for Greek olive oil when the price increases from €3.00 to €3.60 per litre.
parts.markscheme:
• Calculate percentage change in quantity demanded: [(180 − 200) ÷ 200] × 100% = −10%.  
• Calculate percentage change in price: [(3.60 − 3.00) ÷ 3.00] × 100% = 20%.  
• Final answer: PED = −10% ÷ 20% = −0.5.

parts.content: Assume the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) in Greece is 0.75. If there is an injection of €1.5 billion in government spending, calculate the total increase in Greece’s national income using the Keynesian multiplier.
parts.markscheme:
• Calculate multiplier: 1 ÷ (1 − 0.75) = 4.  
• Multiply the injection by the multiplier: 1.5 billion × 4 = €6 billion.  
• Final answer: €6 billion.

parts.content: Using information from Figure 1, calculate the change in the total value of Greece’s shipping exports from 2019 to 2021.
parts.markscheme:
• Total value in 2019: US$5,000 × 50 million = US$250 billion.  
• Total value in 2021: US$6,000 × 52 million = US$312 billion.  
• Final answer: The change is US$312 billion − US$250 billion = US$62 billion.

parts.content: Using information from Figure 1, calculate the percentage increase in the volume of containers exported by the Greek shipping industry between 2019 and 2021.
parts.markscheme:
• Change in volume: 52 million − 50 million = 2 million.  
• Percentage increase: (2 million ÷ 50 million) × 100% = 4%.  
• Final answer: 4%.

parts.content: Define the term “progressive tax.”
parts.markscheme: • A progressive tax is a tax system in which the fraction of income paid as tax rises as income increases.

parts.content: Using an aggregate demand and aggregate supply (AD/AS) diagram, explain how an increase in government spending might affect real output in Greece.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/aa339d68-f21e-46db-95f9-23de75f71a59)
• An increase in government spending raises aggregate demand, shifting the AD curve to the right.  
• The resulting intersection with the short-run aggregate supply curve leads to a higher level of real output and possibly a higher price level.  
• The shift from AD1 to AD2 moves the equilibrium from a lower output (Y1) to a higher output (Y2).  
• This increase in real output can help Greece address its high unemployment rate, given that government spending has a multiplier effect on national income.

parts.content: Using information from Table 2, explain how income inequality might act as a barrier to Greece’s long-term economic growth.
parts.markscheme:
• The Gini coefficient has risen from 0.331 in 2019 to 0.337 in 2021, indicating growing inequality.  
• Higher inequality can limit many individuals’ access to education and job opportunities, reducing labor productivity and innovation in the long term.  
• Significant portions of the population may experience lower living standards, weakening total demand and creating barriers to sustained growth.  
• Lower-income groups find it difficult to improve skills or invest in small businesses, which reduces productivity growth over time.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and knowledge of economics, recommend a policy which could be implemented by the Greek government to reduce unemployment.
parts.markscheme:
• Unemployment can be defined as the proportion of the labor force that is actively seeking work but remains without a job.  
• Expansionary fiscal policy can address high unemployment, especially when real GDP figures indicate economic downturns, as shown by Greece’s negative growth in 2020.  
• Increases in government spending—targeted at infrastructure and tourism-related projects—can boost aggregate demand, shifting the AD curve rightward and raising national output. 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/c643367a-0c90-418a-9cca-00475df3a274)
• This policy uses the multiplier effect, with the marginal propensity to consume at 0.75 in 2021, implying that each euro of government spending can generate multiple euros in total economic activity.  
• Targeting the tourism sector, which contributes 20% of GDP, can create direct and indirect jobs, helping lower Greece’s relatively high unemployment rate of 16.3% in 2021.  
• Direct support for skill development, focusing on areas like hospitality and technology, can reduce structural unemployment and enhance the potential for long-term growth.  
• Lower unemployment provides additional tax revenue and reduces government spending on unemployment benefits, helping to stabilize government finances in the medium term.  
• Higher employment can reduce the risk of widening income disparities, as indicated by the rising Gini coefficient, contributing to stronger, more balanced growth.  
• If sustained, these measures can raise government debt in the short run, but improved employment and GDP growth may bolster tax revenues in the future to counterbalance these costs.  
• This policy approach can promote sustainable job creation, stimulate aggregate demand, and address persistent unemployment, making it advisable as a means of reducing joblessness in Greece.

questions.specification:
Greece is a country in southeastern Europe that has faced significant economic challenges over the past decade. Despite recent signs of recovery, with the economy expanding in 2022 after a prolonged recession, Greece continues to experience relatively high unemployment and concerns about income inequality. Tourism remains a major contributor to GDP, accounting for around 25% of economic activity, while shipping generates about 21% of the country’s export revenue. Another prominent export is olive oil, widely recognized for its quality in the global market.

The Greek government imposes a progressive income tax system, with tax rates rising at higher income brackets. While attempts have been made to strengthen tax collection processes, tax evasion and inefficiencies still pose challenges. Moreover, consumption is subject to a 24% value-added tax (VAT) on most goods and services, although some essential items enjoy reduced rates.

In 2022, Greece’s real GDP growth rate reached 5.9%, supported by a strong rebound in tourism and improving global economic conditions. Nonetheless, the unemployment rate remains elevated at 12.9%. Income distribution, measured by a Gini coefficient of approximately 0.33, suggests moderate inequality, prompting policymakers to consider fiscal and structural measures to reduce disparities. The data below provide insights into the country’s economic performance and market structures.

Table 1: Greek Macroeconomic Indicators (2018–2022)

| Year | Real GDP (billion €) | Nominal GDP (billion €) | Unemployment Rate (%) | Real GDP Growth Rate (%) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 2018 | 173 | 181 | 19.3 | 1.9 |
| 2019 | 176 | 185 | 17.3 | 1.7 |
| 2020 | 165 | 168 | 16.4 | -6.3 |
| 2021 | 172 | 180 | 14.7 | 4.2 |
| 2022 | 182 | 193 | 12.9 | 5.9 |

Table 2: Market Data for Greek Olive Oil Exports to the EU

| Price per litre (€) | Annual Quantity Demanded (million litres) | Annual Quantity Supplied (million litres) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 4.00 | 110 | 95 |
| 4.50 | 105 | 100 |
| 5.50 | 95 | 105 |

Table 3: Selected Income Tax Brackets in Greece

| Annual Income Bracket (€) | Marginal Tax Rate (%) |
| --- | --- |
| 0 – 10,000 | 9 |
| 10,001 – 20,000 | 22 |
| 20,001 – 30,000 | 28 |
| 30,001 – 40,000 | 36 |
| Above 40,000 | 44 |

Table 4: Income Distribution in Greece (selected data)

| Income Group | Percentage Share of Total Income (%) |
| --- | --- |
| Top 10% | 23 |
| Next 30% | 34 |
| Bottom 60% | 43 |
| Gini Coefficient | 0.33 |

Figure 1 (hypothetical): Estimated Income and Consumption Multiplier Effects  
(Note: Depicts a positive relationship between government spending and subsequent changes in real GDP, with an estimated multiplier between 1.2 and 1.4.)

parts.content: With reference to Figure 1, explain how the Keynesian multiplier might affect Greece’s real GDP following an increase in government expenditure on infrastructure.
parts.markscheme:
• An initial injection of government spending on infrastructure generates additional rounds of spending by firms and households, multiplying the initial effect on aggregate demand.  
• Figure 1 shows a multiplier between 1.2 and 1.4, indicating that each euro spent leads to a greater overall increase in national income.  
• As people earn more from the spending, they consume more, creating further demand for goods and services.  
• This cycle results in a final increase in real GDP greater than the original government injection.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and your knowledge of economics, recommend a policy that the Greek government could implement to reduce the relatively high unemployment rate. Justify your recommendation.
parts.markscheme:
• Unemployment can be defined as the portion of the labor force that is actively seeking work but unable to find a job.  
• A suitable policy to reduce unemployment is an expansionary fiscal policy that focuses on infrastructure projects and targeted subsidies for industries such as tourism and shipping.  
• Higher government spending on infrastructure can shift aggregate demand to the right, as indicated by the data showing strong multiplier effects (Figure 1), enabling a larger final increase in real GDP.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/095ef7bd-b9b9-4c78-8476-444c948f2d7d)
• Funding improvements in tourism facilities and port infrastructure builds on the 25% GDP share from tourism and 21% export revenue from shipping, creating new jobs in construction, services, and complementary sectors.  
• Improved infrastructure is likely to attract more visitors, raising demand for labor in tourism-related businesses, helping to reduce the 12.9% unemployment rate reported in 2022.  
• The policy targets Greece’s high unemployment by directly creating construction and service jobs, leading to more consumption and investment, supported by the moderate Gini coefficient (0.33), which indicates room for spending to lower inequalities.  
• There is a risk of higher budget deficits and potential inefficiencies, but stronger tax collection and a progressive taxation system help finance these projects, countering the concern about growing public debt.  
• Some structural reforms in labor markets could accompany fiscal expansion, reducing labor inefficiencies and tax evasion issues noted in the text, maximizing the employment impact.  
• The anticipated result is a virtuous cycle of job creation, higher household incomes, and additional tax revenue that can fund further development, addressing concerns of persistent joblessness.  
• Overall, an expansionary fiscal policy directed toward improving tourism and shipping infrastructure, coupled with efficiency improvements in tax collection, reduces unemployment while leveraging Greece’s core economic sectors.

questions.specification:
Benin is a West African country with an estimated population of 12.5 million in 2022. Its economy is heavily reliant on agriculture, with cotton being the most important cash crop. Cotton alone accounts for roughly 25% of real GDP and over 40% of export earnings. Benin’s service sector, including transportation and trade at the Port of Cotonou, also plays a significant role in its economy.

In recent years, real GDP growth has been relatively strong, supported by public investment and higher agricultural output. Nonetheless, many people continue to live below the poverty line, and income inequality remains a concern. To address fiscal and developmental challenges, Benin’s government has been reforming its tax system, although the inefficiency of revenue collection remains an obstacle. Plans are underway to expand infrastructure at the Port of Cotonou, in the hope of further boosting economic activity.

Table 1: Macroeconomic Data for Benin (2019–2022)

| Year | Real GDP (CFA francs billions) | Real GDP Growth (%) | Inflation Rate (%) |
|------|--------------------------------|---------------------|--------------------|
| 2019 | 8,000                          | 5.5                 | 1.0                |
| 2020 | 8,400                          | 5.0                 | 2.0                |
| 2021 | 9,000                          | 7.2                 | 3.3                |
| 2022 | 9,700                          | 6.5                 | 4.0                |

Table 2: Income Inequality and Population Data (Latest Estimates)

| Indicator                                               | Value          |
|---------------------------------------------------------|---------------:|
| Gini Coefficient                                        | 0.42           |
| Population below national poverty line (%)              | 38             |
| Literacy rate for population aged 15+ (%)               | 52             |
| Estimated population                                    | 12.5 million   |

Table 3: Benin’s Tax System (2022)

| Type of Tax              | Rate                             |
|--------------------------|----------------------------------|
| Corporate income tax     | 30%                              |
| Personal income tax      | Progressive, top rate at 45%     |
| VAT (standard rate)      | 18%                              |

Figure 1: Market for Cotton in Benin  
• At a price of US$0.60 per kg, quantity demanded is 3 million bales of cotton, and quantity supplied is 2.7 million bales.  
• At a price of US$0.65 per kg, quantity demanded falls to 2.8 million bales of cotton, while quantity supplied increases to 2.9 million bales.  

(Assume one “bale” is a standardized unit of cotton output.)

Furthermore, the government recently announced a US$200 million infrastructure project to expand the Port of Cotonou. Economists estimate that the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) in Benin is 0.8, suggesting that the Keynesian multiplier effect of the project could be substantial. In addition, a recent policy discussion proposes raising VAT on luxury goods from 18% to 25%, aiming to increase tax revenues and reduce income inequality.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and your knowledge of economics, recommend one policy that the government of Benin could implement to reduce the negative impacts of income inequality. Justify your recommendation.
parts.markscheme:
• A policy that can reduce negative impacts of income inequality is increased government spending on public education and literacy programs.  
• Income inequality refers to an unequal distribution of income within a population; it is visible in Benin’s 0.42 Gini coefficient and 38% poverty rate.  
• Public education spending raises overall skill levels among low-income groups, addressing the 52% literacy rate and improving long-term productivity.  
• In economic theory, such spending on merit goods shifts the long-run aggregate supply (LRAS) curve outward as the workforce becomes more skilled, raising potential output.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/e4e7c3d8-877c-4cbd-92dd-24b89947f6c8)
• The data show that Benin’s strong reliance on agriculture and the service sector could benefit from better-educated workers and more equitable access to training, improving competitiveness and contributing to higher real GDP growth.  
• The multiplier effect from government spending (with an MPC of 0.8) may further stimulate real GDP and employment, especially if the spending is targeted at low-income groups with higher marginal propensities to consume.  
• The policy can reduce chronic poverty by enhancing human capital, improving future earning potential, and narrowing the income gap, leading to broader economic development.  
• Potential drawbacks include the opportunity cost of diverting funds from other sectors, but improving education is likely to offer a more sustainable long-term impact on inequality.  
• In the context of Benin’s ongoing fiscal reforms, a focus on strengthening revenue collection and using it effectively for educational investment can lower inequality and foster development.  
• Overall, this approach aligns with addressing the root causes of inequality by equipping the broader population with skills and knowledge, thereby boosting productivity and ensuring more equitable economic gains over time.

questions.specification:
Estonia is a small, open economy located in Northern Europe. Since joining the European Union in 2004 and adopting the euro in 2011, Estonia has experienced consistent economic growth. Strong investment in technology has positioned Estonia as a pioneer in digital services, with many government services offered online. Timber remains an important export, and the country has recently implemented progressive taxation measures to address income inequality.

Table 1: Selected Macroeconomic Indicators for Estonia (2018–2021)

| Year | Real GDP (billion euros) | Real GDP Growth (%) | Gini Coefficient | Government Spending (% of GDP) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 2018 | 26.1 | 4.1 | 0.31 | 39.5 |
| 2019 | 27.8 | 3.0 | 0.31 | 40.2 |
| 2020 | 26.8 | -3.6 | 0.32 | 43.0 |
| 2021 | 29.6 | 10.6 | 0.31 | 41.0 |

• In 2021, the Estonian government also increased infrastructure spending by €200 million.  
• The marginal propensity to consume (MPC) in Estonia is estimated at 0.8.  

Table 2: Domestic Market for E-Service Applications in Estonia

| Price (€/month) | Quantity Demanded (thousands of subscriptions) | Quantity Supplied (thousands of subscriptions) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 20 | 100 | 80 |
| 25 | 85  | 85 |
| 30 | 70  | 92 |
| 35 | 60  | 100 |

Figure 1: Estonian Timber Market (hypothetical)  
Between 2020 and 2021, the price of timber increased from €100 per cubic meter to €120 per cubic meter, causing Estonian timber exports to decrease from 40 000 cubic meters to 35 000 cubic meters.

| --- | --- |
| 0 − 14 000  | 0  % |
| 14 001 − 25 000  | 20 % |
| 25 001 − 40 000  | 25 % |
| Above 40 000      | 30 % |

parts.content: Using information from Table 2, calculate the price elasticity of demand (PED) for e-service applications in Estonia as the price increases from €20 to €25 per month.
parts.markscheme:
• Percentage change in price = ((25 − 20) / 20) × 100% = 25%  
• Percentage change in quantity demanded = ((85 − 100) / 100) × 100% = −15%  
• PED = (−15%) / (25%) = −0.6 (in absolute value, 0.6)

parts.content: Using the data in Table 1, calculate the approximate real GDP growth in euros (the increase in real GDP, in billions of euros) from 2020 to 2021.
parts.markscheme:
• Real GDP in 2020 = €26.8 billion; Real GDP in 2021 = €29.6 billion  
• Increase in real GDP = €29.6 billion − €26.8 billion = €2.8 billion

parts.content: Referring to Figure 1, calculate the price elasticity of supply (PES) for Estonia’s timber exports as the price changes from €100 per cubic meter to €120 per cubic meter.
parts.markscheme:
• Percentage change in price = ((120 − 100) / 100) × 100% = 20%  
• Percentage change in quantity supplied = ((35 000 − 40 000) / 40 000) × 100% = −12.5%  
• PES = (−12.5%) / 20% = −0.625 (in absolute value, 0.625)

parts.content: Using information from the text, calculate the total Keynesian multiplier effect on real GDP if the Estonian government increases its infrastructure spending by €200 million, assuming the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) is 0.8.
parts.markscheme:
• Multiplier = 1 ÷ (1 − 0.8) = 5  
• Total effect on real GDP = €200 million × 5 = €1 000 million (or €1 billion)

parts.content: Define the term “progressive tax.”

parts.content: Using information from Table 3, explain how progressive taxation might help reduce income inequality in Estonia.
parts.markscheme:
• Individuals earning higher incomes (above €25 000) face higher marginal tax rates, taking a larger proportion of their additional income.  
• This structure narrows after-tax income differentials because higher earners contribute a progressively larger share.  
• Lower-income groups benefit from lower tax rates, potentially increasing their disposable income proportionately more.  
• The overall effect is a reduction in income inequality, as measured by measures like the Gini coefficient.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and knowledge of economics, recommend a policy which the Estonian government could implement to support further economic growth while addressing income inequality.
parts.markscheme:
• Define “economic growth” as a sustained increase in a country’s real output or real GDP over time.  
• Define “income inequality” as an unequal distribution of income within a population, often measured by the Gini coefficient.  
• Consider that Estonia is a small, open economy with growing digital services and an emphasis on exports like timber, indicating a reliance on global markets.  
• Recognize that income inequality can persist despite growth; progressive taxation already exists, yet supplementary measures may be necessary to address income disparities.  
• Propose an expansion of targeted education and training programs to enhance technological and digital skills, building on Estonia’s strengths in digital services.  
• Argue that improved labor force skills lead to higher productivity and competitiveness of exports, such as e-service applications, driving economic growth.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/03a13168-fd09-47f3-8e73-31e727eb8089)
• Emphasize that equipping lower-income groups with better skills increases their potential earnings, reducing income inequality.  
• Note that the government could fund these programs through existing progressive tax revenues, ensuring that higher earners contribute a larger portion to finance inclusive growth.  
• Suggest that improved skills and higher employment would support government revenues in the long run, creating a virtuous circle of growth and reduced inequality.  
• Conclude that targeted investment in human capital is consistent with the existing digital infrastructure, addresses income imbalances, and strengthens Estonia’s position in international markets.

parts.content: Using real-world examples, evaluate the view that production subsidies increase the social welfare of the domestic economy that introduces them.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:
### Definition

- **Production Subsidy**: A payment by the government to firms to reduce their costs of production, leading to an increase in output and often lower market prices.  
- **Social Welfare**: The overall well-being of society, measured through indicators such as consumer and producer surplus, employment levels, and income distribution.  

### Economic Theory

- Production subsidies shift the supply curve to the right (or downward from the producer’s perspective) by lowering costs, leading to an increase in the equilibrium quantity.  
- Consumer surplus may rise if prices decrease; consumers can purchase more at lower prices, improving affordability and increasing total economic benefit.  
- Producer surplus typically increases as firms receive extra revenue from subsidies, potentially enhancing profitability and investment capacity.  
- Employment may grow in subsidized industries because firms can expand production due to reduced costs and increased demand for output.  
- Government spending on subsidies represents an opportunity cost; resources allocated to subsidies could have been invested elsewhere (e.g., infrastructure or education) potentially yielding higher long-term returns.  
- Potential distortion of comparative advantage occurs if domestic producers become reliant on subsidies, possibly leading to inefficiencies, misallocation of resources, or lower incentives to innovate.
- In the long run, if subsidies encourage overproduction (e.g., in agriculture), there can be negative externalities such as environmental damage or surplus disposal problems.  

### Diagram

- A standard market diagram showing a rightward shift of the supply curve due to the subsidy.  
- Annotate changes in equilibrium price and quantity, highlighting the new consumer surplus, producer surplus, and government expenditure areas.  

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/d1cd9264-c744-4e8b-944b-da31a95fa72f)

### Evaluation  

- Short-run impact:  
  - Positive: Lower prices and higher output can benefit consumers. Producers gain from additional revenue, which may translate into higher employment through expanded production and investment.   
  - Negative: Pressure on government budgets if subsidy programs are large, as funds are diverted from other public services potentially reducing quality or availability of essential services.  

- Long-run impact:  
  - Positive: Industries can strengthen over time, potentially leading to growth in exports and higher domestic employment by improving competitiveness and productivity. 
  - Negative: Over-reliance on subsidies may lead to inefficiency, reduced competitiveness without government support, and possible retaliatory measures from other countries.  

**Real World Examples**  

- The United States spends over US$20 billion annually on agricultural subsidies; this has supported farmers’ incomes and stabilized food prices but has drawn criticism for market distortion.  
- The European Union’s Common Agricultural Policy allocates around €58 billion each year, ensuring stable producer income and food security; however, it has led to oversupply in certain commodities and higher taxes for funding.  
- China’s subsidy program for solar panel manufacturing boosted domestic production capacity and global competitiveness, yet some trading partners alleged dumping and imposed tariffs.  

### Conclusion  

- Production subsidies can raise consumer and producer (U.S agricultural subsidies, EU's Unions Common Agricultural Policy) surplus in the short run and create jobs, contributing to higher social welfare.  
- In the longer term, prolonged dependence on subsidies may reduce efficiency, strain government resources, and distort global trade, thereby limiting competition and innovation, limiting the sustainable gains to social welfare.

parts.content: Using real-world examples, evaluate the effectiveness of administrative barriers in reducing imports.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

### Definition
- **Administrative Barriers**: Non-tariff measures imposed by governments that increase the bureaucratic or legal requirements for imports, such as licensing, product standards, and customs procedures.
- **Imports**: Goods or services produced abroad and sold domestically.

### Economic Theory
- Administrative barriers function as a form of protectionism by raising the transaction costs of foreign producers. They include stringent regulations (e.g., safety standards), extensive paperwork, and complex customs requirements that slow or restrict entry.
- When these barriers increase costs, the supply curve for imports may shift upward (or become more inelastic), reducing the quantity of imports in the market and potentially increasing the market price.
- Domestic producers can benefit from reduced foreign competition, because import barriers limit rivals’ market access, potentially gaining market share and higher profits in the short term.
- Consumers, however, face higher prices or less choice, given that imported goods become more expensive or less available. This can decrease overall consumer welfare.
- In the long run, protectionism via administrative barriers might reduce incentives for domestic producers to innovate or become more efficient, risking global uncompetitiveness over time.
- Other nations may retaliate with similar measures, to restore their competitive balance, potentially leading to trade disputes and reduced exports for the country imposing the barriers.

### Diagram
- A supply and demand diagram for an imported good can illustrate how additional costs caused by administrative barriers shift the import supply curve to the left (or upward), reducing the equilibrium quantity of imports and raising prices.

### Evaluation
- Short-run impacts:
  - Positive: Domestic producers benefit from less foreign competition, which can maintain or increase output and employment in protected industries. Government may gain political support from these industries.
  - Negative: Consumers face higher prices due to increased import costs, and import-dependent businesses may see higher costs and less variety in intermediate goods.
- Long-run impacts:
  - Positive: Some domestic industries can strengthen if they use the temporary protection to build competitive advantages, thus to invest in quality and productivity improvements.
  - Negative: Persistent administrative barriers can impede foreign technology transfer, reduce global competitiveness for domestic firms, and risk retaliatory measures.

**Real World Examples**
- In the mid-2010s, the European Union established rigorous health and labeling requirements for agricultural imports, resulting in a drop in US soybean exports by over 20% in certain years. Domestic EU producers benefited from increased market share initially, but consumers paid higher prices.
- Japan’s complex testing and certification standards for foreign automobiles increased administration costs for foreign carmakers, reducing imports during the 2010s. This supported Japanese manufacturers in the short term, yet global automakers subsequently circumvented some barriers by creating local joint ventures in Japan, indicating partial adaptation and limited long-run impacts on import levels.
- India’s documentation requirements for electronics imports in 2019 delayed shipments and raised importer costs, leading to fewer imported consumer devices in the short run. Over time, local producers did not fully meet consumer demand, and higher market prices persisted, highlighting the longer-term burden on consumers.

### Conclusion
- Administrative barriers can be effective in reducing imports in the short term (such as Japan's increased costs on foreign car-makers), protecting domestic producers and providing them with a competitive edge.
- However, persistent use of such measures can elevate consumer prices, stifle domestic innovation, and provoke retaliatory actions. The overall effectiveness depends on the ability of domestic industries to utilize the protection for long-term competitiveness and the extent to which consumers and other sectors bear the cost.

questions.specification:
## Cambodia’s Economic Transition

Cambodia, located in Southeast Asia, has experienced significant economic transformation over the past decade. Prior to 2018, its economy relied heavily on the garment sector and agriculture, but more recently, rapid growth in tourism, microfinance, and construction has contributed to gross domestic product (GDP) expansion, which averaged about 6.8% annually from 2018 to 2021. Despite this robust growth, pockets of poverty remain, and the government has promoted both infrastructure development and targeted social welfare policies to reduce rising income inequality.

One of the main drivers of growth is the garment industry, accounting for over 65% of total merchandise exports in 2021. However, environmental concerns have arisen due to waste disposal and water pollution from garment factories. In response, the Ministry of Environment has tightened factory inspection procedures and is considering a tradable (emissions) permits scheme for large industrial polluters. Although Cambodia’s capacity to implement environmental regulations is modest, proponents argue that clearer standards could reduce negative externalities and encourage industries to adopt greener technologies.

Tourism has also become a vital component of the economy, comprising about 26% of total service exports before recent global downturns affected international travel. In 2021, over 1.3 million visitors arrived, a sharp decline compared to prior years, but the authorities anticipate a rebound as global travel normalizes. To diversify income, the government supports small and medium enterprises (SMEs) through microfinance programs, focusing on rural households engaged in hospitality or artisanal crafts. Critics maintain that high microfinance interest rates can trap borrowers in debt, underscoring the need for regulatory oversight and financial literacy programs.

Monetary policy in Cambodia is complex, as the local currency (riel) circulates alongside the US dollar. Over 70% of transactions are dollarized, reducing the National Bank of Cambodia’s ability to influence the money supply. Nonetheless, the government has periodically intervened in currency markets to limit volatility in the riel exchange rate. Inflation rates declined from a peak of 3.8% in 2019 to 2.7% in 2021, partly due to global factors and stable domestic demand.

Fiscal policy has emphasized infrastructure, particularly rural roads and modernizing the energy grid. Officials claim these investments will enhance productivity and attract foreign direct investment (FDI), which reached US$3.2 billion in 2021. However, concerns persist about rising external debt levels, mainly financed by bilateral loans. The government maintains some subsidies on electricity and fertilizer to support agricultural producers, but budget pressures have led to debates over gradually phasing out these price supports to fund more targeted social programs.

In terms of international trade, Cambodia benefits from tariff reductions under the Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN) framework. Recent bilateral trade agreements with China and regional economies are expected to further increase Cambodia’s exports of rice, textiles, and electronics. Yet, local businesses claim that non-tariff barriers, such as administrative requirements for exporters, hinder their competitiveness. Advocates call for streamlined procedures and better infrastructure at ports, which could lower trade costs and boost export diversification.

Income inequality, reflecting urban-rural disparities, remains a concern. The official Gini coefficient stood at 0.34 in 2018, then rose slightly to 0.36 by 2021. The government introduced a minimum wage in the garment sector to raise real incomes for laborers, sparking debates on whether higher labor costs might deter investment. Proponents argue that robust growth and rising productivity can accommodate moderate wage increases without jeopardizing competitiveness. Meanwhile, rural regions continue to rely on agriculture, and recurring floods pose challenges to harvests.

Going forward, Cambodia’s economic trajectory depends on balancing social welfare with fiscally sustainable spending. Policymakers must weigh the costs and benefits of further debt accumulation, the possible implementation of environmental taxes, and persistent attempts to manage the exchange rate. If the nation effectively addresses its infrastructure gaps and diversifies away from garments and tourism alone, Cambodia could see inclusive, long-term development.

**Table 1: Cambodia’s Selected Macroeconomic Indicators (2018–2021)**

| Indicator                  | 2018 | 2019 | 2020 | 2021 |
|----------------------------|------|------|------|------|
| Real GDP Growth Rate (%)   | 6.7  | 7.1  | 3.1  | 2.9  |
| Inflation Rate (%)         | 2.3  | 3.8  | 3.1  | 2.7  |
| Unemployment Rate (%)      | 0.7  | 0.7  | 0.9  | 1.1  |
| FDI Inflows (US$ billion)  | 2.8  | 2.9  | 2.5  | 3.2  |
| Government Debt (% of GDP) | 30.1 | 31.5 | 33.0 | 37.8 |

**Table 2: Cambodia’s Export Distribution by Sector (2021)**

| Sector                     | Export Value (US$ billion) | % of Total Exports |
|----------------------------|----------------------------|--------------------|
| Garments and Footwear      | 7.6                        | 65                 |
| Tourism Services           | 3.0                        | 26                 |
| Agricultural Products      | 1.2                        | 10                 |
| Electronics and Machinery  | 0.6                        | 5                  |
| Other Manufacturing        | 0.4                        | 3                  |

parts.content: Define the term “tradable permits” mentioned in the text (Paragraph 2).
parts.markscheme: Tradable permits are rights to pollute that are issued by the government and can be bought and sold in a market, creating an incentive for firms to reduce emissions.

parts.content: List two functions of microfinance in Cambodia (Paragraph 3).
parts.markscheme:
- Providing small loans to rural households and SMEs for investment in artisanal crafts or hospitality.  
- Offering credit and financial tools aimed at poverty reduction and entrepreneurial development.

parts.content: Using information from Table 1, calculate the percentage increase in Cambodia’s government debt (as a share of GDP) from 2018 to 2021
parts.markscheme:
Step-by-step percentage calculation:  
- Government debt in 2018 = 30.1% of GDP  
- Government debt in 2021 = 37.8% of GDP  
- Absolute increase = 37.8 – 30.1 = 7.7 percentage points  
- Percentage increase = (7.7 ÷ 30.1) × 100 ≈ 25.6%

parts.content: Sketch a business cycle diagram to illustrate what might happen to real GDP growth when global tourism demand falls abruptly (Paragraph 3).
parts.markscheme: When global tourism demand falls abruptly, real GDP growth may move from an expansionary phase to a contractionary phase in the business cycle. In a business cycle diagram, the economy would shift leftward from near or at a peak toward a downturn/possible recession if tourism exports fall significantly, reducing aggregate demand and slowing real GDP growth.

parts.content: Using an AD/AS diagram, explain how infrastructure investments in roads and energy could affect Cambodia’s potential output in the long run (Paragraph 5).
parts.markscheme:
- Infrastructure investments (e.g., roads, energy grids) can improve productivity and lower business costs, shifting the long-run aggregate supply (LRAS) curve to the right in an AD/AS diagram. 
- Over time, the economy’s potential output increases, as better infrastructure attracts investment (FDI) and fosters more efficient production. 
- This rightward shift of LRAS reflects higher productive capacity, reducing bottlenecks and promoting sustainable growth.

parts.content: Using an exchange rate diagram, explain how government intervention to limit volatility might affect the exchange rate in Cambodia (Paragraph 4).
parts.markscheme: Government intervention to limit riel volatility may involve buying or selling the riel in foreign exchange markets. In an exchange rate diagram (with the riel on the vertical axis and quantity of riel on the horizontal), central bank purchases of riel would push its demand curve rightward, appreciating or stabilizing its value. Alternatively, selling riel would shift the supply curve rightward, pushing the value down if the government wants to limit large appreciations. The intervention aims to maintain a more stable riel exchange rate.

parts.content: Using a negative externalities diagram, explain why regulators are considering a tradable emissions permits scheme for Cambodia’s industrial polluters (Paragraph 2).
parts.markscheme: A tradable emissions permits scheme addresses the negative externalities of industrial pollution by internalizing costs. In a negative externality diagram, the marginal social cost (MSC) lies above the marginal private cost (MPC). Tradable permits restrict total emissions, creating a market price for pollution rights. Firms face higher marginal costs if they pollute more, aligning private costs with social costs and reducing the welfare loss associated with pollution.

parts.content: Using a Lorenz curve diagram, explain how increases in the minimum wage could impact income inequality between urban and rural households in Cambodia (Paragraph 7).
parts.markscheme:
- An increase in the minimum wage can reduce the share of income going to low-paid urban laborers relative to higher earners if rural households do not benefit equally, potentially shifting the Lorenz curve. 
- If increased wages in the garment sector improve income for lower-skilled urban workers, the curve can become less bowed, indicating reduced inequality among urban low-wage workers. However, rural incomes may lag without parallel policy support, creating new gaps if agricultural areas remain unaddressed.

parts.content: Evaluate the extent to which reducing subsidies and pursuing trade liberalization could lead to sustainable and inclusive growth in Cambodia.
parts.markscheme:
**Definition:**

 - Subsidies: Government financial support aiming to reduce production costs or lower prices for consumers.  
 - Trade liberalization: Reduction or removal of barriers to trade, such as tariffs and non-tariff barriers.  
 - Sustainable growth: Economic expansion that meets present needs without compromising future generations’ capacity to meet their own needs (often involving balanced use of resources, debt sustainability, and environmental considerations).  
 - Inclusive growth: Growth that benefits a broad range of society, reducing income inequality and promoting shared prosperity.

**Range of Arguments:**

- Argument 1: Efficiency Gains from Reducing Subsidies  
  - Lower subsidies, for instance on electricity and fertilizer (Paragraph 5), can reduce budgetary pressures and free resources for targeted social programs.  
  - This can foster more efficient resource allocation, as producers respond to market signals rather than artificially lowered costs.  
  - Potential downside includes higher costs for agricultural producers and possible higher consumer prices, which may disproportionately affect poor rural households.  
  - In Table 1, rising government debt (from 30.1% to 37.8%) indicates budget pressure, suggesting that reducing subsidies could help manage debt but risks short-term social costs.

- Argument 2: Reallocating Government Funds Towards Infrastructure/Social Welfare  
  - Releasing funds from subsidies allows more public investment in infrastructure (Paragraph 5), such as roads and energy, which boosts long-run aggregate supply and potential output (as in part (c)).  
  - Enhanced infrastructure could benefit both rural and urban areas, generating positive externalities and potentially reducing income inequality, by improving mobility, access to services, and economic opportunities.  
  - Evaluation might highlight short-term political resistance to subsidy removal, and the pace of new infrastructure implementation might be slow, delaying inclusive benefits.

- Argument 3: Trade Liberalization Enhancing Export Opportunities  
  - Cambodia already benefits from ASEAN tariff reductions (Paragraph 6), and further trade liberalization could expand exports of rice, garments, electronics, etc.  
  - This may draw more FDI (Table 1 shows US$3.2 billion in 2021) and support job creation, contributing to inclusive growth if employment opportunities are widespread.  
  - However, non-tariff barriers (Paragraph 6) and poor rural infrastructure might limit gains, potentially widening the gap if rural areas cannot compete or connect to export markets, due to higher transaction costs and limited market integration.

- Argument 4: Sustainability Considerations  
	- Improvements in environmental regulation, such as tradable permits (Paragraph 2), and reliance on targeted social programs can support sustainable and more equitable development.  
  - Over-reliance on garments and tourism is risky; diversification via trade liberalization reduces vulnerability to external shocks (Paragraph 8).  
  - A rapid shift away from subsidies without adequate safety nets could raise inequality if rural or low-income households lose support. Balancing fiscal responsibility with social protection is key for sustainability and inclusivity.

- Argument 5: Balancing Debt and Inclusive Growth  
  - As government debt rose to 37.8% of GDP in 2021 (Table 1), removing subsidies can help fiscal sustainability. This fosters better long-term planning for social and environmental programs.  
  - Critics argue that immediate removal may hurt small farmers dependent on fertilizer subsidies, possibly exacerbating rural poverty.  
  - Careful phasing out of subsidies and targeted interventions (Paragraph 5) can mitigate short-term hardships and promote inclusive growth.

**Evaluation and Conclusion:**

  - Reducing subsidies can free state resources for infrastructure and social investments, promoting long-term sustainable growth.  
  - Trade liberalization can widen export markets and reduce reliance on a few sectors, supporting diversification and inclusive development.  
  - Potential negatives include short-term inequality spikes, especially for rural populations if subsidy cuts raise costs and trade benefits do not reach all regions, as price shocks and uneven access to opportunities emerge.  
  - The extent of success depends on the government’s ability to gradually transition away from subsidies, develop effective institutions regulating externalities, and implement complementary policies to ensure that the poor benefit from freer trade.

questions.specification:
Colombia is located in the northwestern region of South America. It is the world’s third-largest producer of coffee, accounting for around 10% of global supply. The Colombian National Coffee Growers Federation (FNC) estimates that approximately 500 000 families depend on coffee production for their livelihoods. In 2022, Colombia earned an estimated US $2.5 billion from coffee exports, representing about 8% of the country’s total export revenue.

Although the Colombian economy has shown signs of growth, income inequality remains a concern. With a Gini coefficient of about 0.50 in 2022, Colombia has one of the highest levels of income inequality in Latin America. The government is exploring new social welfare programs and potential reforms to its tax system to address these disparities.

Table 1 shows key macroeconomic indicators for Colombia between 2021 and 2022. It includes data on real GDP, population, income inequality, government spending, and tax revenue.

Table 1: Key Macroeconomic Indicators for Colombia, 2021–2022

| Indicator                                    | 2021                 | 2022                 |
|---------------------------------------------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Real GDP (billion US$, constant 2015)       | 300                  | 330                  |
| Population (millions)                       | 49.5                 | 50.3                 |
| Gini coefficient                            | 0.51                 | 0.50                 |
| Official poverty rate (%)                   | 35                   | 34                   |
| Government spending (billion US$)           | 60                   | 63                   |
| Tax revenue (billion US$)                   | 52                   | 55                   |

Between January and July 2022, the price of Arabica coffee rose significantly, from US $1.20 per pound to US $1.50 per pound, partly due to strong global demand and weather-related supply constraints. Table 2 shows the changes in price, quantity demanded by the international market, and quantity supplied domestically in Colombia during this period (quantities are measured in millions of standard 60-kg bags of coffee).

Table 2: Price and Quantity Changes in the Colombian Coffee Market, Jan–Jul 2022

| Month    | Price (US$/lb) | Quantity Demanded (millions of 60-kg bags) | Quantity Supplied (millions of 60-kg bags) |
|----------|----------------|-------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------|
| January  | 1.20           | 5.0                                       | 4.0                                       |
| July     | 1.50           | 4.7                                       | 4.3                                       |

In 2022, Colombia’s inflation rate was approximately 4.5%. The Ministry of Finance is considering increasing government investment in the coffee sector to boost production capacity and generate additional employment. Economists estimate a Keynesian multiplier of 1.4 for new investments in the coffee sector, meaning that each dollar invested could generate an additional US $0.40 in national income.

Meanwhile, there is also debate around Colombia’s tax system. The current corporate tax rate stands at 31%, and personal income tax rates are progressive, peaking at 39% for top earners. The standard VAT rate is 19% on most goods and services, although some necessities have lower or zero VAT.

parts.content: Calculate the price elasticity of demand (PED) for Colombian coffee between January and July 2022.
parts.markscheme:
• Percentage change in quantity demanded = [(4.7 − 5.0) ÷ 5.0] × 100 = ( − 0.3 / 5.0 ) × 100 = −6%  
• Percentage change in price = [(1.50 − 1.20) ÷ 1.20] × 100 = (0.30 / 1.20) × 100 = 25%  
• PED = ( −6% ) / 25% = −0.24 (absolute value 0.24)

parts.content: Assuming the multiplier in the Colombian coffee sector is 1.4, calculate the total increase in real GDP if the government invests an additional US $1 billion in that sector.
parts.markscheme: • Total increase in real GDP = 1 billion US$ × 1.4 = 1.4 billion US$

Using the data in Table 1, calculate the ratio of tax revenue to government spending for Colombia in 2022.
parts.markscheme: • Ratio = 55 billion US$ ÷ 63 billion US$ = 0.87 (or 87%)

parts.content: Define the term Keynesian multiplier.
parts.markscheme: • The Keynesian multiplier is the factor by which an initial injection of spending leads to a greater change in national income.

parts.content: Explain why a rise in coffee prices may not lead to a significant reduction in global demand for Colombia’s coffee.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/f4e98160-6cb1-4a78-a14d-817a85112683)
• Coffee tends to have few close substitutes, making global demand relatively price inelastic.  
• Many consumers consider coffee a necessity, minimizing the impact of higher prices on demand.  
• Colombia’s reputation for high-quality coffee reduces consumers’ willingness to switch to alternatives. 
• Coffee expenditure is a relatively small portion of most consumers’ budgets, so a price change has a limited effect on demand.

parts.content: Using the data from Table 1, explain how a reduction in the Gini coefficient might indicate progress toward economic development in Colombia.
parts.markscheme:
• A decrease in the Gini coefficient from 0.51 to 0.50 signals a more even distribution of income.  
• This suggests that lower-income households may be receiving a larger share of total income.  
• Progress toward greater income equity can improve living standards and reduce poverty, important indicators of economic development.  
• The official poverty rate has fallen from 35% to 34%, consistent with the trend of lower inequality.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and knowledge of economics, recommend a policy that the Colombian government could implement to reduce income inequality while supporting the coffee sector.
parts.markscheme:
• Define income inequality as the unequal distribution of income among individuals or households, highlighted by Colombia’s Gini coefficient of 0.50.  
• Define a subsidy as financial assistance provided by the government to firms to lower their production costs and increase output.  
• Propose a targeted subsidy program for small- to medium-scale coffee producers that decreases production costs and encourages higher output to meet global demand, building on Colombia’s significant coffee export revenues of US \$2.5 billion.  
• Explain that subsidizing coffee farms can shift the supply curve to the right in the coffee market, leading to potentially lower prices, higher production, and increased incomes for coffee-growing families.  
• Describe how increased supply in a key export sector can generate export revenue, feed into higher GDP growth, and utilize the Keynesian multiplier of 1.4, so each US \$1 invested can add US \$0.40 in national income.  
• Highlight that increased incomes for coffee-related households could help reduce the official poverty rate (currently 34%) and narrow income inequality by delivering benefits directly to vulnerable groups dependent on coffee production.  
• Emphasize that a targeted government subsidy can work alongside a slightly more progressive direct tax structure (currently peaking at 39% for top earners) to redistribute gains from the coffee sector and fund social welfare programs.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/25b11567-83ee-4ad6-b083-3bbae1a2b751)
• Reference the Gini coefficient’s decline from 0.51 to 0.50, suggesting that a combination of direct support to farmers and progressive taxation can further reduce inequality.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/6353a38d-bdfe-4209-a3b8-f1e46440a709)
• Note that ensuring government spending remains sustainable at 63 billion US\$ compared with a 55 billion US\$ tax revenue requires careful budgeting to avoid excessive deficits.  
• Conclude that targeted subsidies for coffee producers, combined with complementary tax reforms, can foster inclusive economic growth, support a key export sector, and continue the downward trend in Colombia’s Gini coefficient.

questions.specification:
Poland is a Central European country with a population of 37.7 million (2022). Since joining the European Union (EU) in 2004, Poland has experienced significant economic growth. In 2022, Poland’s real GDP grew by 4.9%, following a 5.9% expansion in 2021. Unemployment remains relatively low at 5.2%. However, inflation has risen to 14.4%. Poland’s Gini coefficient stands at 0.31, suggesting a moderate level of income inequality.

Poland’s primary exports (machinery, vehicles, furniture, and electronic devices) go mainly to Germany, the Czech Republic, the United Kingdom, and France. Although the country has diversified, it still relies heavily on coal for electricity generation (about 70%), raising environmental concerns. The Polish government has begun investing in renewables, particularly wind energy, but continues to face pressure from the EU to reduce carbon emissions.

**Table 1: Poland’s Real GDP Growth and GDP (PLN billions), 2019–2022**

| Year | Real GDP Growth (%) | GDP (PLN billions) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 2019 | 4.1 | 2,200 |
| 2020 | -2.2 | 2,150 |
| 2021 | 5.9 | 2,310 |
| 2022 | 4.9 | 2,420 |

**Table 2: Taxation in Poland (2022)**

| Type of tax            | Rate of tax                                                |
| ---------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- |
| Corporate income tax   | 19 %                                                       |
| Personal income tax    | Progressive tax (17 % up to 85,528 PLN, then 32 %)         |
| VAT                    | 23 %, 8 %, 5 % (depending on products)                     |

**Table 3: Selected Trade Data for Poland in 2022**

| Category                                 | Value in EUR (billion)       |
| ---------------------------------------- | ---------------------------- |
| Exports                                  | 281                          |
| Imports                                  | 284                          |
| Balance of trade (exports − imports)     | –3                           |
| Main export partner: Germany             | 28 % of total exports        |

parts.content: Using an AD/AS diagram, explain how an increase in investment in renewable energy might affect Poland’s macroeconomy in the short run.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/2993389c-f247-4af7-a139-a6e5f431688c)

- Higher investment in renewables increases aggregate demand through the investment component (I) of AD.  
- The AD curve shifts right (AD1 to AD2), leading to a rise in real output (Y to Yp) and the general price level (Pl1 to Pl2) in the short run.  
- This may create greater employment as firms expand production.  
- In the context of Poland, more spending on wind energy and other renewables could stimulate domestic industries and boost growth.

parts.content: Using the information from the text, explain two possible consequences of Poland’s continued reliance on coal for its economic development.
parts.markscheme:
- Reliance on coal at 70% for electricity may cause environmental damage and lead to increased pressure from the EU to reduce emissions, potentially resulting in penalties or reduced funding.  
- Continued coal use can delay investment in renewables, preventing Poland from benefiting fully from cleaner energy sources, possibly reducing long-term competitiveness and risking higher costs if carbon taxes are raised.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and your knowledge of economics, recommend a policy which the Polish government could implement in order to address the rising inflation rate.
parts.markscheme:
- Inflation is a sustained increase in the general price level, currently at 14.4% in Poland, which poses risks to purchasing power and economic stability.  
- A contractionary monetary policy could be recommended, involving an increase in interest rates by the Polish central bank to reduce aggregate demand and dampen inflationary pressure.  
- The implementation of a contractionary monetary policy would lower the aggregate demand curve shifting it inwards (AD1 to AD2), this would lower potential output (Yinfl to Yp) and reducing overall price levels (Pl1 to Pl2).

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/c66ebfda-2702-4247-abae-d94f0ead999f)

- Higher interest rates lower consumption and investment by increasing the cost of borrowing, shifting aggregate demand left and easing pressure on the price level.  
- Policymakers could combine this with prudent fiscal measures (such as lower government spending growth) to avoid fueling excess aggregate demand.  
- Referencing Poland’s growth rate of 4.9% and relatively low unemployment of 5.2%, such a policy might be manageable in the short run without triggering a sharp rise in unemployment.  
- Rising interest rates could lead to a slower expansion, but it would help prevent inflation from eroding real wages and savings.  
- The policy may also strengthen the currency, reducing import prices and further contributing to moderating inflation.  
- The government could complement monetary measures with supply-side policies (e.g., investment in energy diversification) to reduce cost-push inflation from heavy coal reliance.  
- Using data about Poland’s current macroeconomic conditions, the priority is controlling inflation before it becomes entrenched, balancing this against the risk of slowing growth.  
- The recommended action is to adjust interest rates upwards while simultaneously encouraging more renewable energy investment to mitigate long-term inflationary pressures from rising energy costs.

questions.specification:
Malaysia is a rapidly developing Southeast Asian nation with a population of about 32 million people. Over the past decade, the country has experienced steady economic expansion, supported by both the manufacturing and services sectors. However, agriculture remains a significant contributor to export revenues, particularly from palm oil. The government has implemented a range of macroeconomic policies to encourage growth, reduce unemployment, and address income inequality.

According to World Bank estimates, Malaysia’s real Gross Domestic Product (GDP) reached US$338.3 billion in 2021 and increased to US$370.1 billion in 2022 (see Table 1). Rising global demand for palm oil led to higher prices in 2022, boosting export revenue but also creating concerns about over-reliance on a single primary commodity. At the same time, Malaysia’s unemployment rate has fallen from 4.6% to 3.8%, partly due to government-sponsored job placement programs.

The government levies both indirect (sales and service tax of 6%) and direct taxes. Corporate income tax stands at 24%, although there are plans to reduce it to 22% to stimulate greater foreign direct investment (FDI). Progressive personal income taxes range from 1% up to 28%, depending on income brackets (see Table 3).

Recently, the government announced a stimulus package of MYR 20 billion (approximately US$4.5 billion) intended to boost aggregate demand. The marginal propensity to consume (MPC) among Malaysian households is estimated at 0.75, implying that the stimulus may have a significant multiplier effect on national income. Nonetheless, policymakers remain concerned about persistent income inequality, as measured by the Gini coefficient (0.41 in 2021 and 0.40 in 2022), and are exploring additional redistributive measures.

Table 1: Key Macroeconomic Indicators for Malaysia (2021–2022)

| Indicator                     | 2021    | 2022    |
|-------------------------------|---------|---------|
| Real GDP (billion US$)       | 338.3   | 370.1   |
| Real GDP Growth Rate (%)     | 3.1     | 8.7     |
| Inflation Rate (%)           | 2.5     | 3.3     |
| Unemployment Rate (%)        | 4.6     | 3.8     |

Table 2: Malaysian Palm Oil Data (2021 vs. 2022)

| Year | Price (US$/metric tonne) | Quantity Demanded (million tonnes) | Quantity Supplied (million tonnes) |
|------|--------------------------|------------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| 2021 | 620                      | 2.10                               | 1.80                               |
| 2022 | 750                      | 2.00                               | 1.95                               |

Table 3: Income Distribution and Taxation in Malaysia (2022)

| Income Bracket (MYR/year) | Marginal Tax Rate (%) |
|---------------------------|------------------------|
| 0 – 35,000                | 1                      |
| 35,001 – 50,000           | 8                      |
| 50,001 – 70,000           | 14                     |
| 70,001 – 100,000          | 21                     |
| 100,001 – 250,000         | 24                     |
| 250,001 – 400,000         | 28                     |

The government’s intention is to diversify the Malaysian economy further—reducing the reliance on palm oil exports—and to address growing concerns about income inequality through progressive taxation and social welfare programs. Policymakers believe that careful use of fiscal policy (including stimulus measures and adjustments to corporate tax rates) and structural reforms can support sustainable long-term growth.

parts.content: Explain why Malaysia’s reliance on palm oil exports might pose a risk to its long-term external stability.
parts.markscheme:
• Dependence on a single primary commodity can lead to vulnerability if global palm oil prices fall, reducing export revenue.  
• Price volatility in commodity markets can lead to fluctuating foreign exchange earnings, making external accounts unstable.  
• Shifts in consumer preferences or environmental regulations can quickly reduce export demand, harming the balance of payments.  
• Over-reliance on palm oil limits economic diversification, leaving the economy exposed to external shocks.

parts.content: Using information from Table 2, calculate the price elasticity of supply for palm oil in Malaysia when the price increases from US$620 to US$750 per metric tonne. Show your working.
parts.markscheme:
- Percentage change in quantity supplied: [(1.95 - 1.80) ÷ 1.80] × 100 = 8.33%
- Percentage change in price: [(750 - 620) ÷ 620] × 100 = 20.97%
- PES = 8.33 ÷ 20.97 = 0.40
- Price elasticity of supply: 0.40

parts.content: Using information from the text, explain how a falling unemployment rate might affect Malaysia’s long-term economic development.
parts.markscheme:
• Lower unemployment indicates more people in work, raising incomes and improving living standards.  
• A growing workforce can increase aggregate output, promoting higher long-term economic growth.  
• Greater employment opportunities may reduce income inequality if wage earnings rise, aligning with government goals.  
• Higher productivity and investment can result from an expanding labor force, supporting long-term development.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and knowledge of economics, recommend a policy which could be implemented by the Malaysian government to reduce income inequality.
parts.markscheme:
• “Income inequality” and “progressive taxation” represent key terms. Income inequality refers to the uneven distribution of income across the population. A progressive tax system is one in which the tax rate increases as income rises.  
• Higher progressive taxation on upper brackets can generate more government revenue, which can be redistributed through public spending and welfare programs. This can address persistent inequality suggested by the Gini coefficient (0.41 in 2021 to 0.40 in 2022).  
• Increasing tax rates specifically in higher income brackets or closing loopholes ensures that those with greater ability to pay contribute more, while minimizing the burden on lower incomes. Reference to Table 3 shows that the current highest rate is 28%. Adjusting this rate could intensify redistributive effects.  
• Government expenditure funded by additional tax revenue could target essential services such as education, healthcare, and skills training, promoting equal opportunities and reducing the reliance on palm oil for employment. This supports the diversification strategy highlighted in the case study.  
• Allocating part of the increased revenue to direct transfer payments for lower-income individuals could reduce immediate financial difficulties and increase aggregate demand, consistent with the multiplier effect described (MPC of 0.75).  
• In the long run, educating and training the workforce can sustain productivity growth, expanding potential output and reducing the risk of over-reliance on commodities. This aligns with the government’s plan for sustainable development and structural reforms.  
• This approach would tackle income inequality by directly redistributing income, helping lower-income groups while maintaining economic stability. An increase in their disposable income also supports domestic industries and encourages growth, further consolidating the long-term external stability sought by policymakers.  
• The policy integrates well with current fiscal measures and works alongside programs that promote job placement, potentially lowering unemployment and supporting broader-based growth.  
• Reducing inequality in this manner could help maintain social cohesion, ensuring that the benefits of economic expansion are shared more evenly.  
• The recommended policy uses progressive taxation as a direct tool to achieve equity, which is consistent with Malaysia’s emphasis on reducing the Gini coefficient and developing a more resilient economy.

questions.specification:
**Australian Solar Industry Faces Trade Barriers to Counter Chinese Imports**

The Australian renewable energy sector has experienced a surge in installation costs over the last six months following the implementation of new trade barriers on imported photovoltaic (PV) solar panels. Solar panels, a critical component in Australia’s transition to net-zero emissions, have become more expensive, leading to concerns among homeowners, large-scale developers, and climate policy experts regarding the pace of the energy transition.

The Australian Anti-Dumping Commission recently determined that several international manufacturers, primarily from China, had been dumping solar panels into the Australian market at prices significantly below their normal value. This practice was found to be causing material injury to the domestic manufacturing sector, prompting the Australian government to introduce protective measures.

Initially, a flat tariff of 35% was applied to certain solar imports. This was later adjusted to a flexible tariff mechanism, which activates if the price of imported panels falls below a government-mandated reference floor price. This policy aims to ensure that domestic manufacturers can compete on a level playing field.

Reports from the energy sector suggest that higher component costs have squeezed the profit margins of installation firms. A spokesperson for the Clean Energy Council (CEC) remarked:
"While we support fair trade, these increased costs are hitting residential rooftops and large-scale industrial arrays alike. We are particularly concerned about the impact on community energy projects and remote micro-grids that rely on low-cost hardware to be viable."

The trade measures were strongly advocated for by AusSolar Ltd, the last major solar panel assembly firm operating in Australia. AusSolar, which maintains production lines in Adelaide and Townsville, had previously warned that without intervention, it would be forced to outsource its entire operation, resulting in the loss of 150 high-tech manufacturing jobs.

Interestingly, in the year following the tariff introduction, AusSolar has added 45 new positions to its technical team. However, market analysts caution that protecting a single domestic firm from competition may lead to higher long-term prices for Australian consumers and potentially slower technological adoption.

Policy advisors are currently debating how to balance industrial protection with Australia's climate objectives. One proposal suggests using the collected tariff revenue to fund research and development in next-generation battery storage, while others argue the funds should subsidize solar installations for low-income households to mitigate the price increases.

Table 1: Impact of Tariffs on Solar Installation Costs in Australia

| Installation Type     | Pre-Tariff Cost (AUD) | Post-Tariff Cost (AUD) | Increase in Cost (AUD) |
|-----------------------|-----------------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Residential (5kW)     | 6,000                 | 6,240                  | 240                    |
| Commercial (30kW)     | 25,000                | 26,500                 | 1,500                  |
| Industrial (500kW)    | 120,000               | 127,200                | 7,200                  |

Table 2: Solar Panel Imports and Domestic Production in Australia

| Year | Solar Imports (Megawatts - MW) | Domestic Production (Megawatts - MW) | Tariff Rate (%) |
|------|--------------------------------|--------------------------------------|-----------------|
| 2021 | 400                            | 80                                   | 0               |
| 2022 | 350                            | 120                                  | 35              |
| 2023 | 310                            | 160                                  | Flexible        |
