parts.content: Using an AD/AS diagram, explain how an increase in government spending might affect the short-run equilibrium level of output in Indonesia.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/22f797ba-0dbd-4944-9764-54d18fafbc1e)
• An increase in government spending shifts the aggregate demand curve to the right because government spending is a component of aggregate demand.  
• In the short run, the rightward shift in AD leads to a higher equilibrium output and a higher price level.  
• Firms increase production to meet the higher level of aggregate expenditure, reducing unemployment.  
• The diagram would show AD shifting right, intersecting the short-run aggregate supply curve at a higher output and a higher price level.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using information from Table 2, calculate the percentage change in Indonesia's coffee exports between 2021 and 2022. Show your working.
Using information from the text above, explain one reason why Indonesia’s Gini coefficient might decrease over time.
parts.markscheme:
• Rising government spending on infrastructure projects can generate employment and income for lower-income groups, reducing income inequality.  
• The progressive personal income tax system charges higher-income earners proportionally more, redistributing income and narrowing the gap.  
• These measures can improve opportunities for the less affluent segments of the population.  
• As the unemployment rate declines (Table 1) and more individuals earn wages, income distribution becomes more equal, contributing to a lower Gini coefficient.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and your knowledge of economics, recommend a policy which could be implemented by the government of Indonesia to reduce the unemployment rate.
parts.markscheme:
• Define key terms such as unemployment and fiscal policy. Unemployment is the situation where individuals who are willing and able to work at the current wage rate cannot find employment. Fiscal policy involves the use of government spending and taxation to influence aggregate demand and macroeconomic outcomes.  
• Explain how expansionary fiscal policy can shift aggregate demand to the right, creating more jobs. When government spending rises, firms increase output to meet higher demand, potentially reducing cyclical unemployment. 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/84d4f6c5-61a8-418b-9701-dc7889d630f2)
• Refer to the case study by noting Indonesia’s infrastructure investment of 30 trillion IDR and an MPC of 0.75, indicating that additional government spending would be multiplied in the economy, increasing employment.  
• Recommend increasing targeted infrastructure projects in rural and urban areas that hire significant labor, directly addressing high unemployment by opening new positions for construction and related services.  
• Highlight that this policy is consistent with Indonesia’s existing focus on infrastructure and would stimulate growth in sectors such as manufacturing and services, as improved infrastructure lowers production costs and raises the potential for investment.  
• Emphasize that the country’s progressive tax structure and VAT system can help fund the increased government spending, relying on higher-income earners and consumption taxes to offset budgetary pressures.  
• Evaluate potential drawbacks: increasing government expenditures might strain the budget, potentially raising public debt or requiring higher taxes in the future. There is also a possibility of demand-pull inflation if the economy nears full capacity.  
• Counterbalance the drawbacks with potential benefits from reduced unemployment, higher aggregate demand, and improved equity, as more people join formal employment.  
• Embed the requirement to address long-term structural unemployment by investing in education and vocational training as part of the infrastructure budget, enhancing the workforce’s employability.  
• Conclude by stating that expansionary fiscal policy focusing on job-creating infrastructure and human capital development, supported by the progressive tax system, can reduce unemployment and promote sustained economic growth in Indonesia.

questions.specification:
Angola is located in southwestern Africa and remains heavily dependent on the oil sector, which accounts for over 50% of its gross domestic product (GDP) and more than 80% of its export revenues. Although the government has sought to diversify the economy by investing in agriculture and other sectors, agriculture still constitutes under 12% of GDP. Angola experiences high levels of income inequality, with a Gini coefficient of approximately 0.51.

From 2019 to 2022, real GDP fluctuated due to changes in global oil prices and the impact of Covid-19. Table 1 shows Angola’s real GDP in selected years (values in US$ billions).

Table 1:  
| Year           | Real GDP (USD billions) |
|----------------|-------------------------|
| 2019           | 88                      |
| 2020           | 84                      |
| 2021           | 85                      |
| 2022 (est.)    | 87                      |

Major food products, such as maize, are partly met through domestic production but still rely significantly on imports. Table 2 shows data on maize demand at two different price levels:

Table 2:  
| Price of Maize (Kz/kg) | Quantity Demanded (metric tons/month) |
|------------------------|---------------------------------------|
| 200                    | 500                                   |
| 220                    | 480                                   |

In 2019, the Angolan government introduced a 14% Value Added Tax (VAT). Corporate income tax stands at 30% while personal income tax is progressive, up to 25% for higher-income earners. Table 3 summarizes the main tax rates:

Table 3:  
| Type of Tax                    | Rate of Tax           |
|--------------------------------|-----------------------|
| Corporate Income Tax           | 30%                   |
| Personal Income Tax            | Progressive up to 25% |
| Indirect Tax (VAT)             | 14%                   |

According to Angola’s finance ministry, each additional 1 Kz of government spending generates an increase of 4 Kz in final national income. Officially, Angola’s unemployment rate is around 6.8%, although many believe underemployment and informal employment are significant.

Although oil remains the dominant export, policymakers are exploring strategies to expand agricultural exports, including maize, to reduce the reliance on imports and diversify the economy.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and knowledge of economics, recommend one policy that the government of Angola could implement to reduce income inequality.
parts.markscheme:
• Income inequality refers to an unequal distribution of income among individuals, measured by the Gini coefficient of about 0.51 in Angola.  
• A plausible policy is to increase government expenditure on public education and vocational training, financed through existing progressive taxation.  
• Expanding education spending supports human capital development and can raise incomes of lower-skilled workers, addressing unequal opportunities arising from heavy reliance on the oil sector.  
• Higher-skilled workers broaden the non-oil economy (agriculture and other sectors), improving productivity and encouraging economic diversification.  
• Diversification of the economy is crucial, as the case study shows that agriculture accounts for under 12% of GDP and oil dominates exports.  
• Greater investment in education enlarges long-run aggregate supply by improving labor quality, potentially lowering long-term unemployment and underemployment. 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/1fa9d232-5a20-40b1-a828-577050fd039c)
• The policy fosters more equitable job opportunities, assisting those in the informal sector and lowering income disparities.  
• A potential drawback is the opportunity cost of diverting government funds, but the finance ministry’s multiplier estimate (1 Kz leading to 4 Kz in income) indicates strong returns to public spending.  
• The policy aligns with the progressive tax structure of up to 25% and corporate tax at 30%, ensuring that higher-income earners fund social investment aimed at reducing inequality.  
• Strengthening human capital and reducing import dependence on key goods can increase growth beyond the oil industry, helping to reduce the Gini coefficient over time.

questions.specification:
**Regional Integration in Bloc X**

**Text A: Regional Integration in Bloc X**
Bloc X comprises five countries with different levels of economic development. In 2018, these countries formed a customs union, removing tariffs on intra-bloc trade and implementing a common external tariff. Since the union's formation:

Intra-bloc trade has increased by 62%.
Exports from Bloc X to external markets have stagnated.
Structural unemployment in less developed member countries has risen.

**Text B: Environmental Concerns**
The removal of tariffs within Bloc X has boosted trade in agricultural products, but increased use of chemical fertilizers has raised concerns about sustainability. External markets have also imposed stricter environmental standards on agricultural imports from Bloc X, potentially reducing demand for these exports.

Table 1: Trade Statistics of Bloc X (2017–2023)

parts.content: Using a circular flow of income diagram, explain how increased intra-bloc trade can influence economic interdependence among Bloc X countries.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/51609e88-a968-4f00-9dcd-13301127c15c)

- The circular flow of income shows exchanges between households and firms, with injections (e.g. exports) and leakages (e.g. imports) in an open economy.
- Increased intra-bloc trade (+30%) enhances the export-import flow in member economies, becoming a crucial part in firm revenues and household incomes.
- Hence, countries become more economically interdependent, relying on each other for markets and supplies.
- Any economic shocks in one member country can now affect others via trade flows, highlighting interdependence.

<marks>2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks>4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using a tariff diagram, explain how the introduction of a common external tariff by Bloc X affects imports from non-member countries.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/b70e4132-caa0-47c8-9546-7bd419ee00b4)

- A tariff is a tax levied on imported goods. Hence, as the diagram shows, a tariff effectively shifts the world supply curve upward by the tariff amount (Pw to Pw+t), raising the price of imports.
- Hence, with a constant domestic demand (Dd) and domestic supply (Sd), the quantity demanded domestically at this higher Pw+t decreases from Q4 to Q3.
- Simultaneously, the quantity that domestic producers are willing and able to supply at this price increases from Q1 to Q2.
- Hence, as a result, there is a decrease in quantity of goods imported (Q4-Q1 to Q3-Q2).
- Additionally, there is a creation of government revenue and welfare loss through the tariff.

<marks>2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks>4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using a negative externality of production diagram, explain how increased agricultural trade may lead to market failure.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/6ca90229-4c0a-455f-994d-3f9768cecce0)

- The use of chemicals in agricultural production leads to negative production externalities: spillover costs not reflected in the market price from the production of the good. These costs come in the form of environmental concerns.
- Hence, the marginal private costs (MPC) producers pay for the good is lower than the real marginal social cost (MSC) society pays.
- This is reflected in the diagram below. The supply curve represents the marginal private costs (MPC), whereas the marginal social cost (MSC) curve is higher, separated by the spillover benefits.
- The marginal private benefits (MPB) and marginal social benefits (MSB) are represented by the demand curve, assuming no consumption externalities.
- Since allocative efficiency occurs when MSB = MSC, but market equilibrium is dictated by MPB =MPC, the socially optimum quantity ($Q_{opt}$) is lower than the quantity produced by the market ($Q_m$). Therefore, there is overproduction of resources, welfare loss, allocative inefficiency, and hence market failure.

<marks>2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks>4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using information from the text/data and your knowledge of economics, evaluate the impact of regional economic integration on economic development in Bloc X.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

### **Definition**  
- **Regional economic integration**: The process by which countries within a geographic region increase their level of cooperation through the reduction or elimination of trade barriers.  
- **Economic development**: The process of increasing real per capita income while improving living standards and reducing poverty within an economy as whole. 

### **Increased Intra-Bloc Trade and Economic Growth**  
#### **Expansion of Trade Within Bloc X**  
- Since forming a **customs union in 2018**, **intra-bloc exports have risen from US$65B to US$105B**, a **62% increase** (Table 1).  
- The removal of **intra-bloc tariffs** has lowered costs and increased **market access**, boosting trade in goods like **agriculture**, which increased from **US$35B to US$60B**.  
- The increased trade has contributed to **real GDP growth**, rising from **3.4% in 2018 to 4.3% in 2023** (Table 2), supporting **aggregate demand (AD)**.  

#### **Diagram**: AD/AS Model  
- Regional integration shifts the **AD curve rightward** due to increased exports and investment, leading to **higher output (Y1 to Y2)** and potential employment gains in trade-oriented sectors.

#### **Limitations**  
- While **GDP growth has accelerated**, **unemployment rose from 5.8% to 8.5%**, indicating **jobless growth** or **labour market rigidity**, especially in less developed member states.

### **Rising Unemployment and Structural Adjustment Costs**  
#### **Uneven Benefits Across Member Countries**  
- Economic integration can lead to **specialisation** and **industry relocation**, disadvantaging **less competitive economies**.  
- Structural unemployment in **less developed members** may result from **increased competition** and **inability to adapt**, as shown by rising **unemployment from 5.5% to 8.5%** (Table 1).  
- Without sufficient investment in **skills, infrastructure**, and **labour mobility**, regional integration may **exacerbate disparities** within Bloc X.

#### **Limitations**  
- Despite rising unemployment, the **HDI improved from 0.65 to 0.71**, suggesting gains in **education, health**, and **incomes**, possibly driven by integration-related reforms and **FDI inflows**.

### **Attraction of Foreign Direct Investment (FDI)**  
#### **Bloc X as a Unified Market**  
- FDI increased from **US$10B in 2017 to US$24B in 2023** (Table 2), possibly due to **reduced trade barriers**, greater **market size**, and **harmonised external tariffs**, making Bloc X more attractive to investors.  
- FDI brings **capital, technology**, and **job opportunities**, contributing to **long-term development** and infrastructure improvements.  
- FDI-driven growth is also likely contributing to the **declining Gini index (from 42.0 to 40.5)**, indicating **moderating income inequality**.

#### **Diagram**: PPC (Production Possibility Curve)  
- Increased FDI shifts the PPC outward by raising **productive capacity**, especially in capital-intensive sectors.

#### **Limitations**  
- If FDI is concentrated in **more developed members**, it may widen **regional disparities**, limiting development in the **poorest regions** unless balanced by **regional development funds** or **targeted policies**.

### **Environmental Concerns and Sustainability**  
#### **Increased Agricultural Trade and Fertiliser Use**  
- Trade liberalisation led to a **doubling of agricultural exports**, but also raised **environmental concerns** over **chemical fertiliser use** (Text B).  
- **Negative externalities** such as **soil degradation, water pollution**, and **reduced biodiversity** may hinder **long-term development** and **public health**.

#### **External Market Pressures**  
- Bloc X faces **stricter environmental standards** from external trading partners, threatening **agricultural export revenues**, especially since **exports to external markets have fallen from US$80B to US$72B** (Table 1).  
- Failure to meet standards may **limit future access to high-value markets**, impacting export growth and development.

#### **Diagram**: Negative Externalities of Production  
- Without internalising the environmental cost, the market equilibrium (Qm) exceeds the socially optimal output (Qs), causing **welfare loss**.

#### **Limitations**  
- Environmental degradation may be a **short-term cost**, but can be addressed through **green technology investment**, funded partly by **rising FDI inflows** or **environmental regulation** harmonised across the bloc.

### **Overall Evaluation**  
#### **Strengths**  
- Bloc X has experienced **stronger GDP growth**, improved **HDI**, rising **intra-bloc trade**, and increased **FDI**, signalling **progress in economic development**.  
- Income inequality has slightly declined, and **trade integration** has deepened regional cooperation, creating **market scale and investment opportunities**.

#### **Weaknesses**  
- Rising **unemployment** in less developed countries suggests **unequal gains**, requiring **labour market reforms** and **targeted support**.  
- **Environmental degradation** and the **loss of external market competitiveness** due to unsustainable agricultural practices could threaten long-term development.  
- Structural limitations (e.g., poor infrastructure, education gaps) may prevent full realisation of **development gains from integration**.

#### **Conclusion**  
Regional integration in Bloc X has driven **GDP growth, FDI inflows**, and improved **human development**, showing positive trends in economic development. However, **rising structural unemployment**, **environmental risks**, and **external trade stagnation** highlight the need for **complementary policies**—such as **labour retraining**, **green investment**, and **inclusive development strategies**—to ensure that regional integration leads to **sustainable and equitable development**.

questions.specification:
Thailand is a middle-income country in Southeast Asia, known for its major industries in tourism, agriculture, and manufacturing. Despite the diversification of its economy, tourism remains a vital source of revenue, contributing around 15% of Thailand’s gross domestic product (GDP). The Thai government has invested heavily in infrastructure projects aimed at boosting tourism and improving the country’s long-term competitiveness.

Thailand’s overall economic performance has shown signs of recovery following global economic uncertainties. Although the pandemic disrupted tourism and trade, Thailand’s export-oriented industries (especially electronics and automotive) have supported growth.

The country experiences disparities in income distribution, with a Gini coefficient of approximately 0.37, reflecting moderate inequality levels compared to regional averages. In 2022, the government introduced targeted measures, including temporary subsidies and public investment, to stimulate aggregate demand.

**Table 1: Macroeconomic Data for Thailand**

| Indicator             | 2019  | 2020  | 2021  | 2022 (Estimated) |
|-----------------------|-------|-------|-------|------------------|
| Real GDP (billion US$)| 505   | 501   | 505   | 530              |
| Real GDP growth (%)   | 2.9   | -6.1  | 1.6   | 2.6              |
| Unemployment rate (%) | 1.0   | 2.0   | 1.9   | 1.5              |
| Gini coefficient      | 0.38  | 0.38  | 0.37  | 0.37             |
| Inflation rate (%)    | 0.7   | -0.8  | 1.2   | 6.0              |

**Table 2: Tourism Data for Thailand (2022)**

| Item                                          | Value                |
|-----------------------------------------------|----------------------|
| Number of international tourists (million)    | 11.2                 |
| Average spending per tourist (US$)            | 1,450                |
| Total tourism revenue (billion US$)           | 16.24                |
| Estimated price elasticity of demand for tourism|
| when price changes from US$1,450 to US$1,530  | -1.2                 |

The Thai government has a progressive tax system and a relatively low corporate income tax rate compared to some neighboring countries. The government’s main sources of tax revenue come from corporate income tax, personal income tax, and a value-added tax (VAT).

**Table 3: Tax Rates in Thailand**

| Type of Tax              | Rate                  |
|--------------------------|-----------------------|
| Corporate income tax     | 20%                   |
| Personal income tax      | Progressive rates up to 35% |
| Value-added tax (VAT)    | 7%                    |

Figure 1 below shows the domestic market for Thai rubber, one of Thailand’s key agricultural exports. Sw represents the world supply, and Dd is the domestic demand. Between 2021 and 2022, the global rubber price (measured in Thai baht (THB) per kilogram) increased from THB 50/kg to THB 60/kg, leading to changes in domestic consumption of Thai rubber. 

Figure 1: Domestic Market for Thai Rubber  
(Price on vertical axis in THB/kg; Quantity on horizontal axis in millions of kg)

parts.content: Using information from Figure 1, calculate the price elasticity of demand for rubber in the domestic market when the price increases from THB 50/kg to THB 60/kg, assuming the quantity demanded changes from 900 million kg to 880 million kg.

- The price elasticity of demand ≈ (–2.22%) / (20%) = –0.11.

parts.content: Using information from the text and data above, explain two ways in which income inequality (reflected in the Gini coefficient) might hinder Thailand’s economic growth prospects.
parts.markscheme:
- Inequality can mean lower overall purchasing power among lower-income groups, potentially reducing consumption and thus dampening aggregate demand, especially when tourism revenues fluctuate (Table 2).  
- Inequality may limit access to education and health services for some groups, reducing human capital development and potentially slowing the country’s shift from agriculture and basic manufacturing to higher-value industries.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and knowledge of economics, recommend a policy which could be implemented by the Thai government in order to support economic growth while managing rising inequality.
parts.markscheme:
- A policy option is to increase public expenditure on education and health care, financed through slightly higher progressive income taxation.  
- Higher progressive taxation reduces the disposable income gap, addressing moderate income disparities indicated by a Gini coefficient of 0.37 (Table 1).  
- Greater access to quality education and health care boosts human capital (increasing overall AD (AD1 to AD2)), supporting productivity gains in key industries such as electronics, automotive, and tourism.  
- Strengthening human capital addresses (which could potentially shift the LRAS curve outwards (LRAS1 to LRAS2)) the risk that pockets of low-income communities cannot participate effectively in the recovery of tourism and the manufacturing sectors.  

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/d9cde380-8b2c-402d-ab51-5c188280aee4)

- Enhanced public services can contribute to long-term competitiveness and economic resilience, aligning with Thailand’s strategy of investing in infrastructure and public projects to stabilize growth (Table 1).  
- Increases in tax revenue enable broader social safety nets, potentially protecting lower-income households from sudden drops in income when tourism receipts or export revenues decline.  
- Emphasis on such programs may stimulate aggregate demand by creating employment opportunities, while simultaneously building long-term supply-side capacity.  
- This can limit the potential negative consequences of inequitable growth, as a larger segment of the population benefits from rising GDP (Table 1).  
- The approach is consistent with expansionary fiscal policy, but carefully targeted to mitigate possible inflationary pressures observed in the rising inflation rate (from –0.8% to 6.0%, Table 1).  
- Over time, the multiplier effect of these expenditures can raise overall economic output by improving productivity and reducing inequality, contributing to sustainable growth.

questions.specification:
Ghana, located in West Africa, has a population of approximately 31 million people. The economy relies heavily on its primary export commodities: cocoa and gold. In 2022, Ghana’s real Gross Domestic Product (GDP) reached GH₵490 billion, compared to GH₵460 billion in 2021, and GH₵430 billion in 2020. Inflation has remained elevated, driven partly by global commodity price increases. Government spending on infrastructure has increased substantially in the last two years. Economists estimate that a recent fiscal stimulus of GH₵600 million, aimed at boosting aggregate demand, could lead to a larger overall impact on national income.  

Ghana’s cocoa industry is one of the largest in the world, with demand influenced by global chocolate consumption. The government also imposes taxes on personal and corporate income, although the tax system struggles with compliance and enforcement. The government is also exploring new policies to reduce income inequality, which remains a challenge despite moderate economic growth.  

Table 1: Ghana’s Macro Data (2020–2022)

| Year | Real GDP (GH₵ billions) | Inflation Rate (%) | Government Spending (GH₵ billions) |  
|-----:|------------------------:|--------------------:|------------------------------------:|  
| 2020 | 430                    | 9.8                | 60                                  |  
| 2021 | 460                    | 10.3               | 65                                  |  
| 2022 | 490                    | 13.1               | 72                                  |  

Table 2: Cocoa Market Changes in Ghana (2022)

| Price per kg (GH₵) | Quantity Demanded (tonnes) | Quantity Supplied (tonnes) |  
|--------------------|---------------------------:|---------------------------:|  
| 13                 | 700,000                   | 550,000                    |  
| 15                 | 680,000                   | 600,000                    |  

Table 3: Ghana’s Tax Brackets (Personal Income)

| Annual Income Range (GH₵) | Tax Rate (%) |  
|---------------------------:|-------------:|  
| 0 – 18,000                | 0            |  
| 18,001 – 36,000           | 10           |  
| 36,001 – 60,000           | 17.5         |  
| Above 60,000              | 25           |  

According to the latest official data, Ghana’s Gini coefficient stands at 0.43, indicating considerable income disparity. While the government aims to reduce inequality, it also seeks to maintain sufficient tax revenue to finance public goods and services.

parts.content: Using the information from Table 1, calculate the rate of real GDP growth in 2022.
parts.markscheme:
• Subtract the 2021 real GDP (GH₵460 billion) from the 2022 real GDP (GH₵490 billion) to find the increase: 490 – 460 = 30 (billion).  
• Divide by the 2021 value and multiply by 100: (30 ÷ 460) × 100 ≈ 6.52%.  
• Final answer: approximately 6.52%.

parts.content: Economists suggest that the GH₵600 million stimulus will increase national income by GH₵2,000 million. Using this information, calculate the Keynesian multiplier for Ghana.
parts.markscheme:
• Use the formula Multiplier = Change in national income ÷ Initial injection.  
• Substitute the values: 2,000 ÷ 600 ≈ 3.33.  
• Final answer: 3.33 (approximately).

parts.content: Using the information from Table 2, calculate the price elasticity of demand for cocoa as the price rises from GH₵13 per kg to GH₵15 per kg.
parts.markscheme:
• Calculate percentage change in quantity (using the midpoint method):  
  %ΔQ = [(680,000 – 700,000) ÷ ((700,000 + 680,000) ÷ 2)] × 100 ≈ –2.90%.  
• Calculate percentage change in price (midpoint method):  
  %ΔP = [(15 – 13) ÷ ((13 + 15) ÷ 2)] × 100 ≈ 14.29%.  
• PED = (–2.90%) ÷ (14.29%) ≈ –0.20 (absolute value 0.20).

parts.content: Using the information from Table 2, calculate the change in total revenue for cocoa producers resulting from the change in price from GH₵13 per kg to GH₵15 per kg.
parts.markscheme:
• Initial total revenue: 13 × 700,000 = GH₵9,100,000.  
• New total revenue: 15 × 680,000 = GH₵10,200,000.  
• Change in revenue: GH₵10,200,000 – GH₵9,100,000 = GH₵1,100,000.

questions.specification:
Finland is a Nordic country with a population of approximately 5.5 million. It has a highly developed economy supported by a robust welfare system and a strong emphasis on research and development (R&D). Finland’s export-oriented industries focus on forest products, technology, and machinery. The public sector is comparatively large, accounting for a significant percentage of total employment and national output. Finland’s tax system is progressive, characterized by both high personal income tax rates and a 20% corporate tax rate.

Table 1 below shows selected macroeconomic indicators for Finland from 2019 to 2022. The Finnish government’s budget balance improved somewhat in 2022, but the country faced relatively higher inflation compared to the pre-2020 period. In 2021, the government expanded its support for research and development, aiming to boost innovation and long-term economic growth.

Table 2 provides data on Finland’s paper exports to the European Union (EU), a significant market for the country’s forest products. Analysts are interested in the price elasticity of demand for Finnish paper to assess how changes in global demand might affect export revenue.

Table 3 outlines a measure of income distribution in Finland, illustrating the nation’s relatively low income inequality. Finland has a Gini coefficient of 0.27, making it one of the more equal countries in the EU. Still, there are ongoing debates about how income inequality and social welfare expenditures affect long-term growth.

Below are three tables with the relevant data:

Table 1: Macroeconomic Indicators for Finland, 2019–2022

| Year | Real GDP Growth (%) | Unemployment Rate (%) | Inflation Rate (%) | Government Surplus/Deficit (% of GDP) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 2019 | 1.1 | 6.7 | 1.0 | -0.9 |
| 2020 | -2.9 | 7.8 | 0.3 | -4.0 |
| 2021 | 3.0 | 7.1 | 2.0 | -2.7 |
| 2022 | 2.4 | 6.7 | 5.0 | -2.0 |

Table 2: Price and Quantity Demanded for Finnish Paper Exports in the EU

| Price (€/tonne) | Quantity Demanded (thousand tonnes) |
| --- | --- |
| 990 | 620 |
| 1,090 | 570 |

Table 3: Income Distribution in Finland, Selected Indicators (2021)

| Indicator                                     | Value   |
|----------------------------------------------|---------|
| Gini coefficient                             | 0.27    |
| Share of income held by top 10%              | 23.5%   |
| Share of income held by bottom 10%           | 2.9%    |
| Estimated government spending multiplier     | 1.5     |

In 2021, Finland introduced an additional progressive tax bracket aimed at higher-income earners. The policy objective was to generate added tax revenue to fund social programs and R&D, while maintaining relatively low levels of income inequality.

parts.markscheme:
• Define “economic growth” as a sustained increase in real output over time and define “income inequality” as the unequal distribution of income within a population.  
• Emphasize that Finland’s current low Gini of 0.27 shows relatively high equality, supported by progressive taxation and welfare systems.

parts.content: Explain how tariffs can affect a market previously operating under free trade.
parts.markscheme:
## Definition:

- **Tariff:** A tax imposed by a government on imported goods, typically aimed at raising the domestic price of these goods.

- **Free trade:** An international trade environment where there are no barriers to trade such as tariffs, quotas, or subsidies.

- **Protectionism:** Government policies designed to protect domestic industries from foreign competition by reducing imports.

## Economic Theory:

- Under free trade, the world price (Pw) is typically lower than the domestic equilibrium price without trade, resulting in imports filling the gap between domestic supply and domestic demand.

- A tariff raises the cost of importing goods. This shifts up the effective supply curve for imported goods, leading to an increase in the domestic market price from Pw to Pw + tariff.

- As the price rises, domestic producers are incentivized to supply more (movement along the domestic supply curve), so domestic production increases.

- Consumers face higher prices and reduce their quantity demanded, leading to lower overall consumption of the qood.

- Part of the decrease in imports is replaced by increased domestic production but overall quantity in the market shrinks, reducing consumer surplus.

- Producer surplus increases because domestic producers receive a higher market price.

- The government gains tariff revenue, calculated by the tariff amount per unit multiplied by the quantity of imports after the tariff is imposed.

- The market experiences a welfare loss, often depicted as two deadweight loss triangles: one showing the loss from reduced consumption and the other from inefficient domestic production at higher costs.

## Diagram:

- Standard import market diagram showing the domestic supply and demand curves intersecting at a higher price once the tariff is imposed.

- Labels for Pw + tariff, the shifted import supply line, new domestic quantity supplied, decreased quantity demanded, and the areas of consumer surplus, producer surplus, and government revenue.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/wDf1Aiy8ZMA9NvojeJNEt.jpeg)

questions.specification:
Finland is a Nordic country with a population of approximately 5.5 million people. It has long maintained high living standards and one of the world’s most comprehensive welfare systems. Exports play a significant role in Finland’s economy, particularly in forestry products (timber, pulp, and paper) and technology equipment. In recent years, Finland’s real GDP growth has fluctuated due to global economic conditions and the COVID-19 pandemic.

Finland is also renowned for having one of the highest per capita coffee consumption rates in the world. Domestic coffee producers face strong competition from international suppliers, yet Finnish consumers display relatively high brand loyalty. In addition, Finland’s top marginal personal income tax rates are steep by international standards, helping to fund generous social programs. However, some policymakers are concerned about maintaining competitive tax rates to attract more foreign direct investment and to stimulate economic growth.

Table 1: Selected Macroeconomic Indicators for Finland (2019–2022)

| Year | Real GDP (billion EUR, 2015 prices) | Annual Real GDP Growth (%) | Government Spending (billion EUR) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 2019 | 208.6 | 1.3 | 57.2 |
| 2020 | 203.8 | -2.3 | 60.1 |
| 2021 | 210.0 | 3.0 | 62.5 |
| 2022* | 214.4 | 2.1 | 63.6 |

*Estimate based on provisional data

Table 2: Income Distribution and Inequality Measures (2021)

| Population (millions) | Gini Coefficient | Top 10% Income Share (%) | Bottom 20% Income Share (%) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 5.5 | 0.27 | 22 | 7 |

Table 3: Coffee Consumption in Finland

| Price (EUR per 500 g) | Quantity Demanded (million 500 g packs per month) |
| --- | --- |
| 4.00 | 10 |
| 5.00 | 9 |

Figure 1: Hypothetical Domestic Demand and Supply for Finnish Forestry Exports
(Price measured in EUR per cubic meter; D and S represent domestic demand and supply functions. Pw indicates the world price. A shift from Pw to Pw1 represents a 10% increase in the world price for forestry exports.)

Assume the following:  
• When the price for forestry exports rises from Pw to Pw1, Finland’s forestry export quantity remains the same (perfectly elastic world demand).  
• The value of forestry exports before the price increase was EUR 8 billion per year.

Finland’s government has recently announced a new social infrastructure program worth EUR 2 billion to stimulate the economy. The marginal propensity to consume (MPC) is estimated at 0.75. Some policymakers argue that Finland should reduce its top personal income tax rate (currently 31% for the national share, excluding municipal taxes) to promote innovation, attract more businesses, and reduce the risk of a slowdown in growth. Others worry that reducing taxes too much could threaten Finland’s commitment to strong social services.

parts.content: Using the information in Table 1, calculate Finland’s real GDP growth rate from 2019 to 2020, showing your working.

The government invests an additional EUR 2 billion in social infrastructure. Given an estimated marginal propensity to consume (MPC) of 0.75, calculate the potential total increase in real GDP resulting from this government spending (the Keynesian multiplier effect).
parts.markscheme:
• Multiplier = 1 ÷ (1 – 0.75) = 1 ÷ 0.25 = 4  
• Total increase in real GDP = 2 billion × 4 = EUR 8 billion

parts.content: Using the data in Table 3, calculate the price elasticity of demand (PED) for coffee in Finland when the price increases from EUR 4.00 to EUR 5.00 per 500 g pack.
parts.markscheme:
• % change in Qd = (9 – 10) ÷ 10 × 100 = -10%  
• % change in Price = (5 – 4) ÷ 4 × 100 = 25%  
• PED = -10% ÷ 25% = -0.4 (in elastic terms)

parts.content: Referring to Figure 1, calculate the percentage increase in the value of Finland’s forestry exports if the world price rises by 10% (from Pw to Pw1).
parts.markscheme:
• Original value = EUR 8 billion  
• New value = 8 × (1 + 0.10) = EUR 8.8 billion  
• Percentage increase = (8.8 – 8) ÷ 8 × 100 = 10%

parts.content: Define the term “progressive tax.”
parts.markscheme: • A progressive tax is one in which the fraction of income paid as tax increases as income rises.

parts.content: Using an AD/AS diagram, explain how the government’s EUR 2 billion social infrastructure investment could affect Finland’s real output and the general price level in the short run.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/abf0f15e-46eb-4d42-ae5b-c83c8e690cfc)
• Government spending is a component of aggregate demand (AD).  
• Higher AD shifts the AD curve to the right in the short run.  
• This leads to an increase in real output and a rise in the general price level.  
• The result is short-run economic growth but also potential inflationary pressure if the economy is near full capacity

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using information from Table 2, calculate the ratio of income share between the top 10% and bottom 20% of Finland's population. Show your working.
parts.markscheme:
- Income share of top 10%: 22%
- Income share of bottom 20%: 7%
- Ratio = 22 ÷ 7 = 3.14
- Ratio of income share: 3.1 (or 3.14)

parts.content: Using the information in Table 2, explain two ways in which income inequality might influence Finland’s macroeconomic performance.
parts.markscheme:
• Lower-income households spend a higher proportion of their income, so inequality could reduce overall consumption and slow aggregate demand if the bottom 20% earn too little to drive spending.  
• High inequality might affect social cohesion and productivity, potentially requiring more public spending to ensure welfare, which could influence government budgets and longer-term growth.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and knowledge of economics, recommend a policy that the Finnish government could introduce to address potential negative impacts of high personal income tax rates on economic growth while maintaining social welfare. Justify the recommendation.
parts.markscheme:
• Definition:  
– “Foreign direct investment” is the long-term investment by multinational corporations in another country’s economy.  
– “Economic growth” is an increase in a country’s real output (real GDP) over time.  
• Explanation/Economic Theory:  
– High personal income tax rates may deter foreign direct investment, as potential investors could perceive lower returns on investment and reduced incentives for innovative activities.  
– Reducing the highest marginal tax rates could raise disposable income for high earners, possibly boosting investment, entrepreneurship, and consumption, which shifts aggregate demand to the right. 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/7cc16cc8-2e4f-4e97-b0c8-686d7fad246b)
– Ensuring social welfare requires sufficient government revenue to fund public goods and services, necessitating a balanced approach so that a decrease in top rates does not undermine essential social programs.  
– A modest reduction in the top marginal rate, combined with maintaining progressive elements and ensuring alternative revenue streams, could uphold social welfare while allowing for a more competitive tax environment.  
• Integrated Case Study References and Evaluation:  
– Finland’s strong social programs rely on tax revenue, so a large cut in top tax rates might compromise government spending (Table 1 shows consistent government spending growth).  
– A slight rate drop could encourage multinational corporations to invest in the technology and forestry sectors, potentially boosting exports and real GDP (which fluctuated due to pandemic conditions).  
– Finland’s commitment to equity is seen in its relatively low Gini coefficient (0.27); any future policy needs to maintain that balance to avoid widening income inequality (Table 2).  
– A cautious reduction in top rates could sustain Finland’s reputation for strong welfare while enhancing competitiveness and preserving brand loyalty benefits in domestic markets (e.g., coffee producers).  
• Recommendation:  
– Introduce a moderate reduction in the top personal income tax rate while compensating with minor indirect taxes or targeted adjustments in other brackets to preserve revenue and maintain social services.  
– Develop incentives for local and foreign firms to invest in key export sectors (forestry, technology), ensuring economic growth while safeguarding public goods funding.  
– Reinforce partial progressivity and maintain a robust social infrastructure to continue supporting incomes at the lower end, limiting any adverse impact on income inequality.  
– This balanced approach can encourage foreign investment and innovation, boost long-term growth, and protect Finland’s strong welfare provisions.

questions.specification:
**Tariff Exemptions Sought on Lithium Carbonate**

The Electric Vehicle Manufacturers Association (EVMA) has petitioned the US Department of Commerce to remove tariffs on imported high-grade lithium carbonate. Lithium carbonate is a fundamental component in the production of lithium-ion batteries, which power the majority of modern electric vehicles (EVs) and consumer electronics.

The US EV battery manufacturing sector employs over 45,000 workers across 18 states. The industry generates approximately US$25 billion in revenue annually. However, the EVMA argues that the current 30% tariff on imported lithium carbonate has significantly increased production costs, making US-made batteries more expensive than those manufactured in East Asia.

A significant supply-demand gap exists in the US lithium market. In 2017, domestic demand was 500,000 metric tons, while domestic production was only 250,000 metric tons, meaning only 50% of demand was met by domestic mining. Since then, while demand has surged due to the EV transition, domestic extraction has struggled to keep pace.

The EVMA has raised concerns about the inconsistent quality of domestically mined lithium and the slow progress of new mining projects. Combined with rising minimum wages for factory workers, higher material costs and quality issues could force battery plants to scale back production, leading to structural unemployment in emerging "battery belt" regions where specialized skills are highly concentrated.

Moreover, the tariff on lithium places lithium-ion batteries at a disadvantage compared to alternative energy storage technologies, such as hydrogen fuel cells and solid-state prototypes, which may not rely on the same taxed inputs. This could shift investment and consumer demand toward alternative propulsion systems, potentially causing a decline in the long-term demand for traditional lithium-ion technology.

Table 1: US Lithium Carbonate Market Data

| Year | Domestic Production (thousand metric tons) | Domestic Demand (thousand metric tons) | Percentage Met Domestically |
|------|-------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------|-----------------------------|
| 2017 | 250                                       | 500                                   | 50%                         |
| 2019 | 260                                       | 540                                   | 48%                         |
| 2021 | 270                                       | 600                                   | 45%                         |
| 2023 | 260                                       | 650                                   | 40%                         |


Table 2: Price and Demand Data for Storage Technologies

| Technology Type       | Average Price per kWh (USD) | Annual Demand (GWh) | Expected Demand Growth Rate |
|-----------------------|-----------------------------|---------------------|-----------------------------|
| Lithium-Ion Battery   | 130                         | 800                 | -2%                         |
| Hydrogen Fuel Cells   | 150                         | 100                 | 12%                         |
| Solid-State Prototypes| 200                         | 50                  | 15%                         |

parts.content: Define the term structural unemployment.
parts.markscheme:
Structural unemployment happens when there is a mismatch between workers’ skills <marks>1 mark</marks> 

and available jobs, often caused by industrial reorganization or technological change. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: List two ways in which tariffs offer trade protection to domestic firms.
parts.markscheme:
- Increase the price of imported goods, making them less competitive compared to domestically produced alternatives. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduce the quantity of imports, allowing domestic producers to gain market share. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Provide revenue for the government which can be used to support domestic industries through subsidies. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Protect infant industries from established foreign competition. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 2 marks max</note>

parts.content: Using information from Table 1, calculate the shortage of lithium carbonate supply in 2023.
parts.markscheme:
Use of formula for calculating shortage of supply and correct value substitution:

$$\text{Shortage} = \text{Quantity Demanded} - \text{Quantity Supplied}$$
$$\text{Shortage} = \text{Domestic Demand} - \text{Domestic Production}$$
$$\text{Shortage} = 650 - 260$$

<marks>1 mark</marks>

Correct result:
$$= 390 \text{ thousand metric tons}$$ (Final Answer)

<marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
Using a market diagram, sketch the impact of the 30% tariff on lithium carbonate on the domestic market for the material.

![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-f348e19d-fbde-4ad1-8a02-d90044efff94-1771840759346/741c7ce6-5ab5-4136-b3ee-dc47f26a5945.jpeg)
parts.markscheme:
- Correctly labeled demand/supply diagram showing world price Pw and the 30% tariff raising price to Pw+t.
<marks>1 mark</marks>

- Correct identification of the tariff revenue or the impact on domestic price/quantity.
<marks>1 mark</marks>

- Showing the reduction in domestic quantity demanded (movement along the demand curve from Q4 to Q3).
<marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
Using a supply and demand diagram and data from the text, illustrate how a disequilibrium arises in the domestic US lithium carbonate market if imports were absent.

![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-f348e19d-fbde-4ad1-8a02-d90044efff94-1771840759346/a6f6b53f-238b-4f47-97b2-910c9d5d0cdb.jpeg)
parts.markscheme:
- Without imports, domestic suppliers provide only 260 thousand metric tons (2023 data) while domestic demand stands at 650 thousand metric tons, creating significant excess demand (shortage).
- As shown in the diagram, domestic supply S is insufficient to meet domestic demand D at the world price, creating a physical shortage of 390 thousand metric tons.
- Market forces in a closed market would push the price significantly higher to clear the market, causing a disequilibrium relative to the international world price Pe.
- This disequilibrium indicates that only 40% of the market demand can be met by domestic producers, as supported by the 2023 data.

<marks>2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks>4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content:
Using a demand and supply diagram, explain how the rising production costs of lithium-ion batteries due to tariffs could affect the market for hydrogen fuel cells.

![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-f348e19d-fbde-4ad1-8a02-d90044efff94-1771840759346/5288f503-1aa9-4edc-8f57-b950ee20dc3d.jpeg)
parts.markscheme:
- The rising costs of lithium-ion batteries (due to lithium tariffs) shift the demand for hydrogen fuel cells from D1 to D2 as they are substitute technologies.
- Manufacturers and automotive firms substitute away from expensive battery technology toward alternatives like hydrogen fuel cells.
- This increased demand for the substitute creates a new equilibrium with a higher price (Pe to P1) and higher quantity (Qe to Q1).
- Table 2 supports this, showing expected demand growth for hydrogen (12%) and solid-state (15%) while lithium-ion faces a -2% growth rate.
- The cross-market effect shows how protectionism in one raw material market can inadvertently subsidize the growth of competing technologies.

<marks>2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks>4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content:
Using a labor market diagram, explain how an increase in the minimum wage of factory workers could lead to structural unemployment in the EV battery industry.

![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-f348e19d-fbde-4ad1-8a02-d90044efff94-1771840759346/a31e0e2d-b131-4c5b-864e-74fa884f4ef2.jpeg)
parts.markscheme:
- An increase in the minimum wage from We to Wmin creates a price floor in the labor market above the equilibrium wage level.
- This wage increase adds to the cost pressures already faced by manufacturers due to the 30% lithium tariff.
- The quantity of labor demanded decreases from Qe to Qd as firms reduce hiring or automate processes to cut costs.
- The resulting labor surplus (Qs - Qd) represents unemployment. This becomes structural because the skills of battery factory workers are specialized and may not transfer easily to other growing sectors.
- With 45,000 workers in the industry, even small percentage cuts in labor demand lead to significant job losses in specific regional "battery belt" hubs.

<marks>2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks>4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content:
Using a subsidy diagram, explain how a government subsidy on domestic lithium mining could address the market failure caused by supply shortages.

![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-f348e19d-fbde-4ad1-8a02-d90044efff94-1771840759346/63fbadb6-8083-4518-b4d2-754be21280cb.jpeg)
parts.markscheme:
- A government subsidy shifts the domestic supply curve for lithium from Sd1 to Sd2 to the right.
- This effectively lowers the production cost for domestic miners, allowing them to increase the quantity supplied from Q2 to Q4 at the same market price.
- Unlike a tariff, a subsidy increases domestic production without artificially raising the input price for battery manufacturers, helping to maintain the competitiveness of the US EV industry.

<marks>2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks>4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using information from the text/data and economic principles, evaluate the view that the tariff will lead to long-term economic growth.
parts.markscheme:
**Answers may include:**

**Definition**  
- Tariff: A trade barrier in the form of a tax on imports, designed to protect domestic industries.  
- Economic growth: An increase in a country's real GDP or productive potential over time.
- Structural unemployment: Unemployment due to changes in the structure of the economy, such as the decline of a specific industry.

**Argument 1: Support for Domestic Mining and Resource Security**  
- The tariff protects domestic lithium miners from cheaper international competition, potentially encouraging investment in US-based extraction.
- Higher prices for imported lithium may increase the producer surplus of domestic miners (as seen in the tariff diagram below), providing funds for technological improvements and quality upgrades.
- In the long term, if domestic production increases from the 260k tons seen in 2023 to meet demand, it could enhance the nation's productive capacity (LRAS) and ensure energy security.
- **Diagram (Tariff Effects)**
![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-f348e19d-fbde-4ad1-8a02-d90044efff94-1771840759346/5576e0e6-ce8f-489c-92ef-d2d27c94d3c8.jpeg)
  - Shows the gain in producer surplus and government revenue which can be reinvested into R&D.

**Argument 2: Negative Impact on Downstream Manufacturing and Innovation**  
- The 30% tariff significantly raises input costs for battery manufacturers. Since batteries are a major cost component of EVs, this reduces the price-competitiveness of US-made electric vehicles.
- Rising costs plus increased minimum wages may lead to a contraction in the battery sector (45,000 jobs), causing structural unemployment. This contraction reduces the industry's contribution to GDP.
- Substitution effects (Table 2) show demand shifting away from lithium-ion (-2% growth) toward hydrogen (12%) or solid-state (15%). If the US industry is locked into lithium-ion but faces high costs, it may lose out on the global transition to the next generation of power storage.
- **Diagram (AD/AS - AD shift)**
![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-f348e19d-fbde-4ad1-8a02-d90044efff94-1771840759346/754aff99-a20f-466b-97f8-ca4e233947ab.jpeg)
  - Reduced investment and consumption in the battery/EV sector leads to a leftward shift in AD, hindering growth.

**Argument 3: Long-term Productive Potential**  
- If the tariff successfully incentivizes a domestic "lithium boom," the resulting increase in infrastructure and specialized labor could shift the LRAS to the right, representing long-term growth.
- However, if the shortage persists (only 40% met domestically in 2023) and manufacturers relocate, the economy loses its manufacturing base, shifting LRAS to the left.
- **Diagram (LRAS shift)**
![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-f348e19d-fbde-4ad1-8a02-d90044efff94-1771840759346/d3e889fd-8f59-4b53-ba97-1ac7a3cdd1d3.jpeg)
  - Shows the theoretical goal of increasing potential output (Ye1 to Ye2).

**Conclusion**  
- While the tariff aims to protect a strategic resource industry, the immediate cost to the much larger battery manufacturing sector and the resulting structural unemployment pose severe risks.
- Given the rapid shift toward alternatives shown in Table 2, the tariff may undermine the very technological leadership required for long-term growth. A more effective policy might involve subsidies or R&D grants rather than restrictive trade measures.

questions.specification:
The Philippines is a lower-middle-income country in Southeast Asia comprised of over 7,600 islands. With a population of approximately 110 million in 2021, the country has historically relied on agriculture, services (particularly tourism and business-process outsourcing), and overseas remittances from millions of Filipinos working abroad. Despite steady economic growth prior to 2020, the Philippines continues to face challenges such as high income inequality and reliance on external revenue sources.

The government has launched various infrastructure projects, aiming to improve roads, ports, and public transport networks—measures intended to stimulate growth and reduce regional disparities. In addition, policymakers hope that better infrastructure will encourage higher foreign direct investment (FDI) and boost employment opportunities.

Remittances remain a significant source of income. Official data indicate that money sent home from migrant workers accounts for around 10% of the country’s GDP. Some economists argue that this reliance on external income might slow the development of more robust domestic industries, though others point to the benefits of improved household spending and higher savings due to remittances.

Table 1 shows selected labor market data for 2021, whereas Table 2 provides nominal GDP and price index data for 2019 and 2021. Table 3 outlines the main tax rates in the Philippines. Table 4 provides information on banana exports, one of the Philippines’ leading agricultural exports.

Table 1: Key labor market data for the Philippines (2021)

| Population (millions) | Employed (millions) | Unemployed (millions) |
|-----------------------:|---------------------:|-----------------------:|
|                   110 |               44.41 |                  3.03 |

Table 2: Nominal GDP and price index (2019 = base year 100)

| Year | Nominal GDP (trillion pesos) | Price index (2018=100) |
|-----:|-----------------------------:|------------------------:|
| 2019 |                        16.8 |                    106  |
| 2021 |                        18.5 |                    110  |

Table 3: Tax rates in the Philippines

| Type of tax            | Rate                       |
|------------------------|----------------------------|
| Corporate income tax   | 25%                        |
| Personal income tax    | Progressive up to 32%      |
| Value-added tax (VAT)  | 12% (on most goods/services)|

Table 4: Banana exports from the Philippines

| Year | Export quantity (million tonnes) | Price (pesos per kg) | Total export revenue (billion pesos) |
|-----:|----------------------------------:|----------------------:|--------------------------------------:|
| 2021 |                             3.60  |                   25  |                                 90.0  |
| 2022 |                             3.40  |                   28  |                                 95.2  |

The Gini coefficient for the Philippines is estimated at about 0.44, suggesting moderate to high levels of income inequality. Meanwhile, government spending on infrastructure—a key policy priority—totaled approximately 300 billion pesos in 2021, intended to stimulate aggregate demand and address the uneven development across the country.

parts.content: Using the data in Table 1, calculate the rate of unemployment in the Philippines for 2021.
parts.markscheme:
- Labor force = 44.41 million + 3.03 million = 47.44 million  
- Unemployment rate = (3.03 ÷ 47.44) × 100 ≈ 6.39%

parts.content: Using the data in Table 4, calculate the price elasticity of demand (PED) for Philippine bananas between 2021 and 2022.
parts.markscheme:
- Percentage change in quantity = ((3.40 – 3.60) ÷ 3.60) × 100 = –5.56%  
- Percentage change in price = ((28 – 25) ÷ 25) × 100 = 12%  
- PED = (–5.56%) ÷ (12%) ≈ –0.46

parts.content: Using the information in Table 2, calculate the approximate real GDP growth rate for the Philippines from 2019 to 2021.
parts.markscheme:
- Real GDP 2019 = 16.8 ÷ (106 ÷ 100) ≈ 15.85 trillion pesos  
- Real GDP 2021 = 18.5 ÷ (110 ÷ 100) ≈ 16.82 trillion pesos  
- Real GDP growth rate = ((16.82 – 15.85) ÷ 15.85) × 100 ≈ 6.12%

parts.content: A local firm in the Philippines purchases office equipment at a net (before VAT) cost of 100,000 pesos. Using the tax data in Table 3, calculate how much VAT the firm must pay on this purchase.
Define the term “progressive tax.”
parts.markscheme: A progressive tax is one in which the proportion of income paid in tax rises as income increases.

parts.content: Explain why reliance on overseas remittances might limit the country’s long-run economic development.
parts.markscheme:
- Reliance on remittances may reduce domestic investment incentives, as households depend on external income instead of local production.  
- This can lead to slower growth of domestic industries, limiting the development of a more diversified economic base.  
- Migration of skilled workers abroad can create a potential brain drain, diminishing long-run productivity.  
- Over time, spending fueled by remittances might not generate sustainable job creation within the local economy.

parts.content: Using the information provided, calculate the value-added tax (VAT) payable on a purchase worth 300 billion pesos of infrastructure-related goods and services. The standard VAT rate in the Philippines is 12%.
parts.markscheme:
Correct formula:
VAT = 12% of 300 billion pesos
= 0.12 × 300 billion = 36 billion pesos

parts.content: With reference to the information provided, explain two ways in which high income inequality might hinder economic development in the Philippines.
parts.markscheme:
- Limited access to education and health services for lower-income groups constrains their productivity, which slows overall economic progress.  
- Concentrated wealth can reduce broader market demand, as significant parts of the population may lack purchasing power, hindering long-term growth prospects.  
- The Gini coefficient of 0.44 indicates moderate to high inequality, which undermines balanced development and slows poverty reduction.  
- Regions with lower incomes might miss out on infrastructure and investment, perpetuating uneven growth.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and your knowledge of economics, recommend a policy that the government of the Philippines could implement to encourage greater export growth and reduce unemployment.
parts.markscheme:
- Exports refer to domestically produced goods and services sold abroad, and unemployment refers to the situation where a portion of the labor force is without work. A recommendation is to lower corporate taxes for export-oriented firms.  
- Reductions in the corporate tax rate from 25% to a lower rate could incentivize both domestic and foreign firms to expand operations in key sectors such as agricultural exports (e.g., bananas) and manufacturing, benefiting from economies of scale.  
- The policy would increase export competitiveness by allowing firms to retain more profits for reinvestment, potentially leading to more jobs and production aimed at foreign markets.
- This can be shown in the diagram by a rightward shift of the SRAS (and possibly AD), leading to higher output and lower unemployment without significant inflation.
 ![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/f4a6545c-b02b-4b26-b124-2c7c26658ad4)
- Higher production for export markets could help reduce unemployment by absorbing more of the 3.03 million unemployed workers, as indicated in Table 1.  
- Greater output could then be sold internationally, aligning with data showing significant potential in agricultural exports (Table 4) and the government’s focus on infrastructure development.  
- Linking the tax incentive to performance criteria (e.g., minimum export targets) would ensure that benefits directly bolster export-led growth and result in sustainable employment gains.  
- With 300 billion pesos designated for infrastructure spending, improved logistics can further reduce production costs and enhance export capacity, reinforcing the positive impact of a tax incentive.  
- Benefits include attracting more foreign direct investment, boosting demand for local resources, and diversifying industries beyond reliance on remittances.  
- A possible challenge involves reduced tax revenue in the short run, limiting public funds for social programs, but higher levels of output and employment could expand the future tax base and offset initial losses.  
- This policy can encourage a more robust export sector, address unemployment, and support the Philippines’ long-run growth objectives by diversifying sources of national income.

questions.specification:
Iceland, with a population of approximately 376 000 in 2022, has a highly developed economy that relies on fisheries, tourism, and aluminum smelting as its main industries. Tourism emerged as the most rapidly expanding sector over the last decade, surpassing fish exports as the largest export earner. However, fish exports remain vital to employment and economic stability.

Despite relatively high standards of living and a strong social welfare system, Iceland’s income inequality has fluctuated. The country’s Gini coefficient has risen and fallen in recent years, partly reflecting changes in taxation policies and variations in household income from tourism. The government imposes progressive personal income taxes, along with corporate and value-added taxes. In 2022, economists in Iceland estimated that the average marginal propensity to consume (MPC) was 0.8.

Table 1: Iceland’s Real GDP and Gini Coefficient

| Year | Real GDP (billion ISK) | Gini Coefficient |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 2020 | 2 490                  | 0.27            |
| 2021 | 2 580                  | 0.29            |
| 2022 | 2 675                  | 0.28            |

Table 2: Price and Quantity Demanded of Fresh Cod in the Domestic Market (2021)

| Price (ISK/kg) | Quantity Demanded (tonnes per month) |
| --- | --- |
| 1 600          | 12 000                               |
| 1 800          | 10 800                               |

Table 3: Selected Tax Rates in Iceland


| Type of tax            | Rate of tax                         |
| ---                    | ---                                 |
| Corporate income tax   | 20 %                                |
| Personal income tax    | Progressive rates up to 46 %        |
| Value-added tax (VAT)  | 24 %, 11 %, 0 % (depending on goods) |

parts.content: Using the information in Table 2, calculate the price elasticity of demand (PED) for fresh cod when the price increases from ISK 1 600 per kg to ISK 1 800 per kg.
parts.markscheme:
• Percentage change in quantity demanded = (10 800 − 12 000) ÷ 12 000 × 100 % = −10 %  
• Percentage change in price = (1 800 − 1 600) ÷ 1 600 × 100 % = 12.5 %  
• PED = (−10 %) ÷ 12.5 % = −0.8 (the absolute value is 0.8)

parts.content: Using Table 1, calculate Iceland’s average annual growth rate in real GDP from 2020 to 2022. Show all working.
parts.markscheme:
• Real GDP in 2020 = 2 490; Real GDP in 2022 = 2 675  
• Growth factor over two years = 2 675 ÷ 2 490 ≈ 1.0743  
• Average annual growth rate = (1.0743^(1/2) − 1) × 100 % ≈ 3.64 %

parts.content: Given that the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) in Iceland is 0.8, calculate the Keynesian multiplier.

Suppose a worker in Iceland earns ISK 6 000 000 per year. Using Table 3’s information (and assuming the maximum marginal personal income tax rate applies fully above a certain threshold), calculate how much personal income tax this worker pays if she faces an effective average rate of 35 %.
Define the term progressive tax.
parts.markscheme: • A progressive tax is one in which the fraction of income paid as tax increases as income rises.

parts.content: Using an AD/AS diagram, explain how growth in net exports from tourism might affect the Icelandic economy in the short run.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/653951ad-987a-48a8-9b76-5bc717f8f934)
• Additional export revenue from tourism raises aggregate demand, shifting AD to the right.  
• Higher AD tends to increase real output (real GDP) and creates upward pressure on the general price level.  
• Rising tourism earns foreign currency and boosts real incomes, promoting consumer spending and stimulating economic activity.  
• In the short run, producers respond by increasing output, which can lead to economic growth and a lower unemployment rate.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Assume the Icelandic government imposes a VAT of 11% on fresh cod. Calculate the final price a consumer would pay per kg if the market price before tax is ISK 1 800.
parts.markscheme:
VAT = 11% of ISK 1 800
 = (11/100) × 1 800 = ISK 198

Final price = 1 800 + 198 = ISK 1 998 per kg

parts.content: With reference to the data in Table 1, explain how changes in the Gini coefficient from 2020 to 2022 might reflect shifts in Iceland’s income distribution.
parts.markscheme:
• The Gini coefficient rose from 0.27 to 0.29 between 2020 and 2021, indicating a temporary widening in income inequality.  
• Between 2021 and 2022 it fell to 0.28, suggesting a reduction in income disparity.  
• These movements could be linked to fluctuations in incomes derived from Iceland’s key exports (fisheries and tourism) and changing taxation policies.  
• A falling Gini coefficient often suggests more even distribution of income, whereas an increasing Gini suggests relatively greater inequality.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and knowledge of economics, recommend a policy which the government of Iceland could introduce in order to reduce the level of income inequality.
parts.markscheme:
• Income inequality describes how unevenly income is distributed, and the Gini coefficient (ranging from 0.27 to 0.29 and then 0.28) measures the extent of that inequality.  
• A progressive tax ensures that as income rises, the proportion of income paid in taxes increases, which can narrow income gaps when properly structured.  
• Additional government spending on services like education or vocational training can raise the incomes of lower-skilled workers, aligning with Iceland’s high MPC of 0.8, so each krona spent potentially circulates multiple times in the economy to benefit lower-income groups.  
• The combination of progressive taxation (up to 46 % for higher incomes) and social spending can reduce disparities, since those who benefit most from strong tourism or fish exports contribute more to government revenues.  
• By channeling these revenues into targeted assistance—such as scholarships for industry-specific courses or subsidies to lower-income fishery communities—greater equity is achieved, consistent with Iceland’s strong social welfare objectives.  
• The approach could help stabilize the Gini coefficient at lower levels, mitigating the fluctuations shown in Table 1 by compensating households facing income volatility from seasonal changes in tourism.  
• The multiplier effect associated with the 0.8 MPC suggests that fiscal transfers or government outlays amplify total spending, further boosting incomes at the lower end and stimulating domestic demand for goods beyond tourism and fisheries.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/e207f85c-4fa7-44e2-9157-c7d6b3012668)
• Iceland’s relatively small population means appropriately scaled measures in education and job support can have a meaningful impact, improving long-term productivity and wage potential.  
• This policy might entail opportunity costs, as funds allocated to training or services could have been used elsewhere, but the social benefits of reducing inequality (more social cohesion, less strain on welfare systems) can justify the expense.  
• A carefully administered policy, using progressive taxes and increased social expenditures, can help Iceland maintain stable growth while ensuring more equitable income distribution.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: 4. The Global Economy > 4.3 Arguments For and Against Trade Protection > 4.3.1 Arguments For Trade Protection
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.3.1 Arguments For Trade Protection
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Increasing domestic producer revenue is a primary objective and argument for trade protection, and the question asks for an evaluation of this specific outcome.

parts.content: Using real-world examples, evaluate the view that import quotas increase domestic producers' revenues.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:
### Definition:

- **Import Quota**: A quantitative limit on the volume of a specific good that can be imported into a country over a given time period.
- **Domestic Producer**: A firm or entity that manufactures goods within the country’s borders, aimed primarily at meeting domestic or export demand.
- **Revenue**: The total income a firm receives from selling its goods or services, calculated as price per unit multiplied by quantity sold.

### Economic Theory:

- Import quotas are a form of trade protection that limit foreign competition by restricting the supply of imported goods.
- With fewer foreign goods in the market, due to trade restrictions, domestic producers face less competition, potentially raising the market price. This reduced competition allows them to charge more, and this higher price can lead to increased revenue for domestic producers, assuming demand remains relatively stable.
- The degree to which domestic producers increase production depends on their spare capacity and ability to meet the excess demand created by the reduction in imports, which previously serving part of the market.
- Domestically produced goods may become relatively more attractive when imports are restricted, shifting consumer expenditure toward domestic goods. If demand is price inelastic, domestic producers can benefit significantly from higher prices. This reduction in allocative efficiency tends to make quotas politically sensitive, especially if the goods are essential.
- However, higher prices can reduce overall consumer surplus, as consumers may pay more for a smaller quantity of goods, resulting in a welfare loss for consumers
- Over time, domestic producers may face less incentive to innovate or reduce costs because of diminished foreign competitive pressure. This could undermine efficiency and competitiveness in the long run.

### Diagram:

- A standard market diagram with demand (D) and domestic supply (S) can illustrate an import quota by showing the vertical limit on the quantity of imports. The quota raises the equilibrium price, aligning with reduced total supply of the product.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/5dc005e3-66b5-4222-8dc6-60aa45ea7bbe)

### Evaluation:

- Short-term impact:
  - Positive: Domestic producers may increase output and enjoy higher revenues due to less foreign competition and higher market prices.
  - Negative: Consumers may face higher prices and limited product variety, as a result of import restrictions, which can reduce purchasing power.
- Long-term impact:
  - Positive: Some domestic industries could grow and gain market share, due to decreased foreign competition, leading to potential employment gains.
  - Negative: Lack of foreign competition may reduce domestic producers’ incentive to invest in productivity and quality improvements, putting them at a disadvantage internationally over time.

**Real World Examples**:

- The US sugar quota has historically protected domestic sugar producers by limiting sugar imports, resulting in higher domestic sugar prices. Domestic producers such as sugar beet farmers benefited from higher revenues, although consumers and food manufacturers faced higher input costs.
- Japan’s quota on certain agricultural products allowed these domestic producers to command higher prices. However, higher food costs led to consumer dissatisfaction and international pressure to reduce protective measures.
- The EU’s Common Agricultural Policy, which includes quota arrangements for some products, has seen initial boosts to domestic farmers’ revenues but has also attracted criticism for inflating food prices and straining trade relationships with non-EU countries.

### Conclusion:

- Import quotas can raise domestic producers’ revenues (US sugar quota) in the short run by limiting foreign competition and allowing higher prices, but they risk dampening long-run efficiency and international competitiveness.
- Whether the increase in domestic producers’ revenues is sustained depends on the ability of these producers to use the protection period to increase efficiency, innovate, and remain competitive once trade barriers are relaxed.
